Trigger on: community building, user group, customer community, member engagement, community programming, Discord, Slack community, community moderation, 1-9-90 rule.
items.author: Harmony

connectors.name: Slack
connectors.reason: Community platform and messaging hub; track engagement through message volume, threads, reactions

connectors.name: Discord
connectors.reason: Alternative community platform; better for large communities and voice channels

connectors.name: HubSpot
connectors.reason: Segment community members by customer tier; identify power users and advocates for recruitment

connectors.name: Google Analytics
connectors.reason: Track community impact: referrals from community, member signup rate, retention of active members

items.prompt:
## Purpose

Community is not a marketing channel. It's a product. Great communities give members identity (you're part of this tribe), belonging (people here understand me), and status (I can grow and be recognized here).

The 1-9-90 rule: 1% create content, 9% contribute/engage, 90% lurk. Design for all three. Lurkers get value from observing (don't force them to participate). Contributors get recognition (replies, reactions, etc.). Creators get status and community leadership.

Communities don't scale with community size. They compound with engagement depth. A 500-person community with 3 posts per week and no moderation is dead. A 50-person community with daily conversations, weekly programming, and clear culture is alive.

Your job is to start small (50 passionate early members), establish culture fast, give members escalating roles, create programming cadence, and moderate relentlessly. The community that compounds is the one where members feel invested.

## When to Use This Skill

- You're launching a community and want to avoid the "empty room" problem
- You have an existing community with low engagement and don't know how to revive it
- You want to measure whether your community is actually creating value (vs. just existing)
- You're considering whether your product even needs a community (not every product does)
- Members have different levels of activity and you want to nurture different roles
- You're seeing member churn in community and don't know why
- You want to predict whether your community will compound or decay

## Instructions

1. **Assess community-market fit before building**
   - Does your product naturally create peer-to-peer value? (Slack: yes. Calculator app: no.)
   - Will members want to share wins and ask questions? (E-commerce: yes. Spreadsheet tool: maybe not.)
   - Can members help each other solve problems? (CMS: yes. Accounting software: maybe.)
   - Community-market fit check: Would you have started this community if it didn't have product benefits?
   - Sub-bullets: Survey 5-10 customers; ask if they'd want to connect with peers; assess product fit for peer learning

2. **Identify and recruit 50 early members (your founding community)**
   - Your first 50 set the culture. Choose them intentionally, not at random.
   - Who to recruit: power users, curious learners, teachers/mentors, influencers in their own networks
   - Avoid: only customers; include non-customers who care about the category
   - Recruitment: personal outreach; "We're starting a community and want you as a founding member"
   - Sub-bullets: Make a list of 50-100 potential founding members; reach out personally to 50 strongest; expect 50% to accept; start with those 25-50

3. **Define community culture and norms early**
   - Culture is established in the first 10 conversations. Decide what you want and exemplify it.
   - Norms include: How helpful vs. critical? How formal vs. casual? Who can post vs. lurk? Moderation approach?
   - Example: "We celebrate wins, help people unblock, and assume good intent. Spam and self-promotion get one warning then removal."
   - Write 3-5 community guidelines and pin them visibly
   - Sub-bullets: Draft guidelines; share with founding members; refine based on feedback; enforce consistently from day 1

4. **Design escalating member roles to encourage growth**
   - Member (default): Can post, comment, lurk. Recognition: listed in member directory.
   - Contributor: Posted helpful content >3 times. Recognition: "Contributor" badge, listed first in directory.
   - Moderator: Trusted community member who handles day-to-day moderation. Recognition: special role, input on community direction.
   - Ambassador: Top contributors who recruit new members or represent community externally. Recognition: highest status; may get perks.
   - Define how people graduate between levels (transparent criteria: "Get 3 upvotes on helpful posts" or "Contribute for 3 months")
   - Sub-bullets: Define roles; communicate criteria; recognize people publicly when they level up; avoid gatekeeping

5. **Create programming cadence (weekly/bi-weekly events)**
   - Empty communities are dead. Regular programming creates rhythm and expectation.
   - Programming types: AMAs (Ask Me Anything with founder/expert), Showcases (members share projects), Challenges (solve problem together), Office Hours (1-on-1 help)
   - Cadence: At least 1 event per week (minimum). 2-3 per week = healthy. Daily = mature community.
   - Who runs it: Mix of founder/team + top community members (so members feel ownership)
   - Sub-bullets: Plan 8 weeks of programming; announce schedule; rotate hosts; measure attendance and engagement

6. **Establish moderation as your immune system**
   - Bad behavior (spam, harassment, tone-deaf pitches) kills culture if not addressed
   - Moderation types: preventative (clear norms), active (watch for rule breaks), responsive (warn and enforce)
   - Response: First offense = warning + education. Second = removal. Consistent enforcement is critical.
   - Give moderators clear tools and authority; don't make them guess
   - Sub-bullets: Define moderation policy; train moderators; establish escalation path; measure moderation load (should be <5% of posts)

7. **Measure engagement depth, not just size**
   - Vanity metric: Total members
   - Real metric: Active members (posted in past 30 days), engaged members (replied or reacted in past week), deep engagement (multiple conversations)
   - Calculation: If 500 members but only 30 post in any month, your community is 94% lurkers
   - Track: Total members, monthly active, daily active, topics/threads per week, replies per thread, member growth rate
   - Sub-bullets: Build dashboard tracking engagement metrics; set targets (e.g., 30% MAU); flag when engagement declines

8. **Design community experiences that compound**
   - Compounding experience: One member's contribution helps 10 others → 10 others feel motivated → 3 contribute back
   - Mechanics: Upvotes/reactions show value, threading keeps conversations alive, search makes old wisdom discoverable, tagging makes expertise visible
   - Non-compounding: Flat chronological feed, no way to upvote, no search, help questions get lost
   - Sub-bullets: Audit your platform for compounding mechanics; add threading/upvotes/tags if missing; test if members help each other

9. **Measure community impact on business metrics**
   - Impact metrics: % of active community members who remain customers (vs. non-members), revenue per community member, NPS of community members
   - Recruitment impact: How many new signups referred by community? % of signup source attribution?
   - Retention impact: Do community members churn less than similar non-members?
   - Cost: How much are you spending on community (time, hosting, events)? ROI = business impact / cost
   - Sub-bullets: Track cohort retention for members vs. non-members; measure referral impact; calculate community ROI

10. **Respond to declining engagement with interventions**
    - Warning signs: Fewer posts per week, fewer new members joining, longer time to first reply, members asking to leave
    - Interventions (in order): Revive programming (missed cadence?), recruit new voices (get fresh members), re-moderate (culture drifted?), rebrand/relaunch
    - Avoid: Blasting members with "please be more active" (makes problem worse); adding too much structure (kills organic growth)
    - Sub-bullets: Monitor engagement weekly; identify decline early; run 1-2 intervention tests; measure impact before escalating

## Output Format

```
COMMUNITY STRATEGY & HEALTH ASSESSMENT
======================================

Product/Community: [Name]
Launch Date (or Current Status): [Date or Status]
Primary Platform: [Slack / Discord / Forum / Other]
Community Size: [Current members]
Analysis Date: [Date]

---

COMMUNITY-MARKET FIT ASSESSMENT

Product Category: [E.g., "Productivity software"]
Peer-to-Peer Value: [High / Medium / Low]
- Can members help each other solve problems? [Yes/No, explain]
- Will members want to share wins? [Yes/No, explain]
- Do customers naturally want to connect with peers? [Yes/No, explain]

Community-Market Fit Verdict: [Strong / Moderate / Weak]
- If Strong: Community is a natural fit; invest in growth
- If Moderate: Community works but needs intentional design
- If Weak: Consider whether community is necessary; may not compound

---

FOUNDING MEMBER RECRUITMENT

Target for Founding Members: [50]
Recruitment Status: [Not started / In progress / Complete]

Founding Member Profile:
- Who: [Persona—power user, teacher, influencer, category expert]
- Why recruit: [How will they shape culture?]
- Expected to contribute: [What will they add to community?]

Recruited Members (so far): [X out of 50]
- Name | Role/Expertise | Recruitment method | Status
- [Actual recruiting progress]

Recruitment Gaps: [Any personas not yet recruited? Any network connections missing?]

---

COMMUNITY CULTURE & NORMS

Founding Culture Statement:
- In one sentence, what's the vibe of this community?
- Formal vs. casual? Generous vs. critical? Sales-free vs. open?

Community Guidelines (Pinned):
1. [Guideline 1]
2. [Guideline 2]
3. [Guideline 3]
4. [Guideline 4]
5. [Guideline 5]

Culture Indicators:
- How members greet newcomers: [Observational—warm? Indifferent?]
- Response time to questions: [Average time to first reply]
- Tone of feedback: [Constructive? Dismissive? Helpful?]
- Moderation style: [Hands-off? Strict? Balanced?]

---

MEMBER ROLES & ESCALATION PATH

[Role 1: Member (Default)]
- Who: [Anyone who joins]
- Criteria to achieve: [Automatic]
- Visibility: [Listed in member directory]
- Recognition: [How are they recognized?]
- Responsibilities: [What are they expected to do?]

[Role 2: Contributor]
- Who: [Members who've helped others or shared valuable content]
- Criteria to achieve: [E.g., "3+ upvoted responses to questions OR 1+ shared project"]
- Visibility: [Contributor badge + featured in announcements]
- Recognition: [Highlighted in monthly newsletter as "Contributor spotlight"]
- Responsibilities: [Encourage continued contributions]

[Role 3: Moderator]
- Who: [Top contributors who help manage community]
- Criteria to achieve: [Must be Contributor + active for 3+ months + nominated by founder]
- Visibility: [Moderator badge, special channel access]
- Recognition: [Monthly meetup with team, input on community strategy]
- Responsibilities: [Watch for rule breaks, help onboard members, host programming]

[Role 4: Ambassador]
- Who: [Top moderators who represent community externally]
- Criteria to achieve: [Must be Moderator + demonstrated leadership]
- Visibility: [Highest status badge, listed on website]
- Recognition: [Invited to company events, given swag, compensation if applicable]
- Responsibilities: [Recruit new members, speak at events, lead signature programming]

Communication of Roles:
- [How will members know about roles and how to level up?]
- [Visibility: pinned doc, onboarding, monthly reminders?]

---

PROGRAMMING CADENCE

8-Week Launch Programming Plan:

Week 1: [Event Type + Topic]
- Host: [Who's running it?]
- Goal: [What are you trying to accomplish?]
- Expected attendance: [X members]

Week 2: [Event Type + Topic]
- Host: [Who?]
- Goal: [What?]
- Expected attendance: [X]

[Repeat for 8 weeks]

Program Types (and frequency):
- AMA (Ask Me Anything): [Frequency—monthly? weekly?] | Host: [Founder / Team / Community member]
- Showcase: [Frequency] | Members share projects, process, learnings
- Challenge: [Frequency] | Community tackles problem together
- Office Hours: [Frequency] | 1-on-1 help or group learning
- Roundtable: [Frequency] | Open discussion on community-suggested topics
- Guest Speaker: [Frequency] | Expert or peer from outside community
- Other: [Type] | [Frequency]

Long-Term Programming Strategy:
- Target event frequency: [Events per week, e.g., 2-3]
- Rotation: [How will you keep it fresh? Vary hosts, topics, formats?]
- Measurement: [How will you measure success of each event?]

---

MODERATION POLICY & TEAM

Moderation Guidelines:
1. [What behavior is encouraged?]
2. [What behavior is tolerated but not encouraged?]
3. [What behavior is not allowed?]
4. [Consequences: warning, removal, escalation?]

Examples of Rules:
- [Self-promotion limit: "1 promotion post per person per month"]
- [Tone: "Assume good intent; offer help instead of criticism"]
- [Spam: "Removed immediately; second account banned"]
- [Off-topic: "Moved to #off-topic or removed if inappropriate"]

Moderation Team:
- [Founder / Team lead]: [Escalations, policy decisions]
- [Moderator A]: [Community name, responsibilities]
- [Moderator B]: [Name, responsibilities]
- [Response time target: X hours for rule breaks]

Moderation Metrics:
- [% of moderation actions per 100 posts]: [Target <2%]
- [Average response time to flag]: [Target <2 hours]
- [Member satisfaction with moderation]: [Target >80% feel fair]

---

ENGAGEMENT METRICS & DASHBOARD

Active Members (Monthly):
- Total members: [X]
- Monthly active (posted/reacted): [X] ([Y]%)
- Weekly active (posted/reacted): [X] ([Y]%)
- Daily active: [X] ([Y]%)

Engagement Depth:
- Posts per day: [X]
- Comments/replies per post: [X.X]
- Threads (conversations): [X per week]
- Likes/reactions per post: [X]

Specific Behaviors:
- % of members who've posted at least once: [X]%
- % of members who've helped someone else: [X]%
- % of members who've attended event: [X]%
- Repeat attendance rate (attended 2+ events): [X]%

Member Growth:
- New members per week: [X]
- Member churn per month: [X]%
- Member satisfaction (net promoter / 1-5 scale): [X]

Target Metrics (Quarterly):
- Monthly active rate: [X]%
- Engagement per member: [X posts + comments per member per month]
- Repeat event attendance: [X]%
- Member growth: [X per month]

Dashboard: [Link to internal dashboard tracking all above metrics]
Review cadence: Weekly (activity), Monthly (engagement depth), Quarterly (strategy)

---

COMPOUNDING MECHANICS & PLATFORM FEATURES

Platform: [Slack / Discord / Forum / Custom]

Features That Drive Compounding:
- [Threading]: Keeps conversations alive; replies roll up
- [Upvotes/reactions]: Shows what's valuable; encourages more of it
- [Search]: Old wisdom stays discoverable; compounds learning
- [Tags/categories]: Expertise becomes visible; members find relevant conversations
- [Profiles]: Members can showcase expertise; build reputation

Features Currently Missing (that would help compound):
- [Feature A]: [Why it would help compound]
- [Feature B]: [Why it would help compound]

Platform Assessment:
- Does platform default to chronological or engagement-weighted? [Impact on compounding]
- Can members upvote/react? [If not, add feature]
- Is there search? [If limited, community knowledge gets lost]
- Are member profiles visible? [If not, you can't build reputation]

---

COMMUNITY IMPACT ON BUSINESS METRICS

Community Cohort Analysis:
- Members vs. non-members retention (month 1): [X]% vs. [Y]%
- Members vs. non-members retention (month 6): [X]% vs. [Y]%
- Churn rate differential: [Members churn X% less than non-members]

Recruitment & Referral Impact:
- % of new signups referred by community members: [X]%
- Referral volume per month: [Y new signups]
- Referral CAC vs. paid CAC: [Community referral is X% cheaper]

Revenue Impact:
- LTV of community members: $[X]
- LTV of non-members: $[Y]
- Uplift: [Community members are X% higher value]
- Upsell rate (free to paid) in community: [X]%

Community Cost:
- Monthly investment: $[X] (team time + platform + events)
- ROI: [Revenue impact / Cost]
- Payback period: [X months]

---

GROWTH & REVITALIZATION PLAN (If declining)

Warning Signs Detected:
- [Engagement metric that's declining?]
- [Member satisfaction issue?]
- [Churn in community?]

Current Status: [Healthy / Stable / Declining / Needs Revival]

If Declining, Interventions (in priority order):
1. [Intervention A + timeline + success metric]
2. [Intervention B + timeline + success metric]
3. [Intervention C + timeline + success metric]

Intervention Examples:
- Revive programming: [Events paused? Restart with weekly cadence]
- Recruit new voices: [Bring in 10 new members with different expertise]
- Re-moderate: [Tighten norms if culture drifted; loosen if too strict]
- Rebrand/relaunch: [New name, new platform, refresh messaging if stale]

Success Metrics for Revival:
- Engagement increases to [X]%
- New member join rate increases to [Y per week]
- Member sentiment improves to [Z]

---

LAUNCH TIMELINE & CHECKLIST

Month 1: Foundation
- [ ] Assess community-market fit
- [ ] Recruit 50 founding members
- [ ] Define culture & guidelines
- [ ] Design member roles & escalation
- [ ] Set up platform + initial moderation

Month 2: Growth & Programming
- [ ] Launch 8-week programming plan
- [ ] Promote 5-10 members to contributor status
- [ ] Share first engagement metrics
- [ ] Gather feedback on what's working

Month 3: Scaling & Optimization
- [ ] Analyze engagement depth; identify top members
- [ ] Recruit moderators from top members
- [ ] Refine programming based on attendance
- [ ] Plan next phase (growth targets, new programs)
```

## Example

```
COMMUNITY STRATEGY & HEALTH ASSESSMENT
======================================

Product/Community: BuildFlow Community (No-Code Automation Platform)
Launch Date: February 1, 2026
Primary Platform: Slack (with #introductions, #wins, #help, #off-topic, #programming)
Community Size: 1,200 members (6 weeks in)
Analysis Date: March 24, 2026

---

COMMUNITY-MARKET FIT ASSESSMENT

Product Category: No-Code Automation / Workflow Tools
Peer-to-Peer Value: High
- Can members help each other solve problems? YES—BuildFlow has infinite use cases; users solving unique problems daily
- Will members want to share wins? YES—No-code builders love to showcase what they built
- Do customers naturally want to connect with peers? YES—Highly correlated with product; power users want to learn from each other

Community-Market Fit Verdict: Strong
- Community is natural fit for product; members create content organically
- Investment in community growth and programming will compound

---

FOUNDING MEMBER RECRUITMENT

Target for Founding Members: 50
Recruitment Status: Complete (recruited 52)

Founding Member Profile:
- Who: Power users (10+ workflows), teachers/community builders, no-code influencers, category experts
- Why recruit: Set tone for helpful, generous culture; ensure early programming quality
- Expected to contribute: 30% to actively participate; 20% to host programming; 100% to help onboard new members

Recruited Members: 52/50 ✓
- Core: 15 power users actively using BuildFlow; recruited via direct outreach
- Builders: 20 no-code community members (Zapier, Integromat forums); recruited via Twitter/LinkedIn
- Teachers: 12 course creators / YouTubers in automation space
- Advisors: 5 former community builders at other companies

Recruitment Gaps: None identified; strong founding group established

---

COMMUNITY CULTURE & NORMS

Founding Culture Statement:
"A community for no-code builders to share workflows, ask for help, and celebrate what they've shipped. No gatekeeping, no spammy pitches, pure learning."

Community Guidelines (Pinned):
1. **Share wins**: Show us what you built and why it matters. We celebrate all shipped workflows.
2. **Help generously**: If someone asks for help, assume they're learning. Guide instead of criticize.
3. **No spam**: 1 promotion per person per month. Self-promotion links get removed; repeat offenders removed.
4. **Respect expertise**: Credit and tag people whose workflows or advice you used. Build in public.
5. **Keep channels focused**: #help for questions, #wins for shipping, #off-topic for off-topic. Use threads for follow-ups.

Culture Indicators:
- How members greet newcomers: Warm and immediate. New members get 5-10 welcome messages + help within 1 hour.
- Response time to questions: 4 min average to first reply (excellent; many members monitoring channels)
- Tone of feedback: Constructive and encouraging. "Here's a better way..." not "That won't work."
- Moderation style: Balanced. 1-2 spam posts removed per week; culture enforces norms organically

---

MEMBER ROLES & ESCALATION PATH

[Role 1: Member (Default)]
- Who: [Anyone who joins]
- Criteria: Automatic
- Visibility: Listed in member directory
- Recognition: Welcome message + pinned as "New member"
- Responsibilities: Can participate, ask, lurk, or share

[Role 2: Helper]
- Who: Members who've answered >3 questions helpfully
- Criteria: 3+ upvoted responses to #help questions (or founder nomination)
- Visibility: Helper badge in Slack profile + featured in weekly "Helpers spotlight"
- Recognition: Name in Slack profile, featured weekly
- Responsibilities: Continue answering questions, mentor others

[Role 3: Builder (Contributed Workflow)]
- Who: Members who've shared a workflow they've built
- Criteria: 1+ workflow shared + 10+ reactions from community
- Visibility: Builder badge + featured in #wins (reposted with highlight)
- Recognition: Monthly "Builder spotlight" email with link to their workflow
- Responsibilities: Share more workflows; mentor others on their techniques

[Role 4: Moderator]
- Who: Top helpers + builders who manage community day-to-day
- Criteria: Must be Helper or Builder + active for 2+ months + nominated by founder
- Visibility: Moderator badge, access to #moderation channel
- Recognition: Weekly 30-min sync with founder; input on programming decisions
- Responsibilities: Watch for spam/rule breaks, help onboard members, host monthly AMA
Current Moderators: 5 (identified from early engagement)

[Role 5: Ambassador]
- Who: Moderators who recruit and represent community
- Criteria: Moderator + demonstrated leadership + referred 5+ members
- Visibility: Ambassador badge, listed on website as "Community Leaders"
- Recognition: Invited to company offsites, exclusive swag, considered for product advisory board
- Responsibilities: Host signature programming, recruit builders, speak at company events
Current Ambassadors: 2

Communication of Roles:
- Pinned doc in #introductions explaining all roles
- Automated "You've been promoted!" message when someone levels up
- Monthly newsletter highlighting new Helpers and Builders

---

PROGRAMMING CADENCE

8-Week Launch Programming Plan:

Week 1 (Feb 1): Founder AMA
- Host: CEO (founding)
- Goal: Set tone for founder accessibility; answer "why BuildFlow exists"
- Attendance: 47 (80% founding members)

Week 2 (Feb 8): Workflow Showcase #1
- Host: Top power user (volunteer)
- Goal: Show what's possible; encourage building
- Attendance: 28 | 15 workflows shared

Week 3 (Feb 15): How to [Build a Lead Scraper] Workshop
- Host: Moderator
- Goal: Hands-on learning; low-code skill-building
- Attendance: 34 | 8 DMs asking for follow-up

Week 4 (Feb 22): Open AMA - Ask the Community
- Host: 3 top builders (panel)
- Goal: Shift authority to community; they're the experts too
- Attendance: 41 | 23 questions answered

Week 5 (Mar 1): Challenge - Build an AI Email Automation
- Host: Moderator (designing)
- Goal: Gamify learning; see what people create
- Attendance: 52 | 12 submissions

Week 6 (Mar 8): Workflow Showcase #2
- Host: Builder (first non-founder/moderator host)
- Goal: Member-led content; scale community voice
- Attendance: 38 | 11 workflows shared

Week 7 (Mar 15): Office Hours - 1-on-1 Help
- Host: Founder + Moderators (rotating slots)
- Goal: Individual support; unblock power users
- Attendance: 22 (18 one-on-ones)

Week 8 (Mar 22): Retrospective - State of BuildFlow Community
- Host: Founder + Moderators
- Goal: Gather feedback; plan next phase
- Attendance: 63 (highest yet; retrospectives resonate)

Program Types (Current & Future):
- AMA (Ask Me Anything): Bi-weekly | Host: Founder, Moderators, Guest Experts
- Showcase: Weekly | Members show workflows; audience votes on best via reactions
- Challenge: Monthly | BuildFlow suggests problem; members build solutions; vote on winners
- Office Hours: Bi-weekly | Founder + Moderators offer 1-on-1 help slots
- Roundtable: Monthly | Community votes on topic; open discussion
- Guest Speaker: Monthly | Bring in no-code influencers / complementary tool experts
- Workshop: Monthly | Deep-dive on specific BuildFlow capability

Long-Term Programming Strategy:
- Target: 3 events per week
- Rotation: Mix founder/moderator/member hosts to scale community voice
- Measurement: Attendance, % new members attending (should be >20%), feedback/sentiment
- Evolution: Transition from founder-led to member-led events (goal by month 6)

---

MODERATION POLICY & TEAM

Moderation Guidelines:
1. Encourage: Sharing workflows, asking questions, helping others, experimentation
2. Tolerate: Off-topic chat (#off-topic channel), light self-promotion (1 per month), debate on best practices
3. Not allowed: Spam links, harassment, competitor self-promotion, misleading claims
4. Consequences: First warning (private message + education), second offense (public warning in channel), third removal (removal + ban)

Examples of Rules:
- Self-promotion limit: "1 promotion post per person per month maximum. Repeat violations result in channel removal."
- Tone: "Assume good intent. If feedback sounds harsh, rephrase as a question or guide instead."
- Spam: "Remove immediately; repeat account flagged for ban. No exceptions."
- Off-topic: "Redirect to #off-topic. Ignore if derailing active conversation."
- Competitor mentions: "OK to mention tools for comparison. Not OK to promote competitor services as better. Judgment call; escalate to founder."

Moderation Team:
- **Founder (CEO)**: Escalations, policy decisions, final authority | Response time: same day
- **Moderator A** (Power User): Community engagement, daily monitoring | Response time: <1 hour
- **Moderator B** (Builder): Workshop facilitation, member support | Response time: <2 hours
- **Moderator C** (Influencer): Content quality, guest recruiting | Response time: <2 hours
- **Moderator D** (Helpful member): Spam flagging, new member onboarding | Response time: <3 hours
- **Moderator E** (Early founder): Community vibe monitoring, culture maintenance | Response time: <1 hour

Moderation Metrics:
- % of moderation actions per 100 posts: 0.3% (excellent; low need)
- Average response time to rule break: 45 minutes (targets <2 hours, outperforming)
- Member satisfaction with moderation: 94% feel fair (measured in retrospective)
- Moderator burnout risk: Low (distributed across 5 people; no one overloaded)

---

ENGAGEMENT METRICS & DASHBOARD

Active Members (Current - Mar 24):
- Total members: 1,200
- Monthly active (posted/reacted in past 30 days): 387 (32.25%)
- Weekly active (posted/reacted in past 7 days): 142 (11.8%)
- Daily active: 28 (2.3%)

Engagement Depth:
- Posts per day: 18
- Comments/replies per post: 2.4
- Threads (conversations): 4 per day average
- Likes/reactions per post: 3.2
- Avg message length: 250 characters (conversational, not DM-like)

Specific Behaviors:
- % of members who've posted at least once: 28%
- % of members who've helped someone else: 19%
- % of members who've attended event: 31% (1,200 total / avg 37 per event)
- Repeat attendance rate (attended 2+ events): 56% of attendees

Member Growth:
- New members per week: ~43 (from 352 at launch to 1,200 in 6 weeks)
- Member churn per month: 2.1% (low; most members stay)
- Member satisfaction (survey, 1-5 scale): 4.3/5 (very satisfied)

Target Metrics (vs. Actuals):
- Monthly active rate: Target 25%, Actual 32% ✓ (outperforming)
- Engagement per member: Target 0.5 posts/reactions per month, Actual 0.7 ✓
- Repeat event attendance: Target 40%, Actual 56% ✓ (high investment in programming paying off)
- Member growth: Target 20/week, Actual 43/week ✓ (compounding faster than expected)

Dashboard: [Slack Analytics + custom Looker dashboard tracking all above]
Review cadence: Weekly activity check, Monthly deep-dive, Quarterly strategy review

---

COMPOUNDING MECHANICS & PLATFORM FEATURES

Platform: Slack (with custom Slack workflow automation)

Features That Drive Compounding:
- **Threading**: Conversations stay organized; follow-up replies roll up. Members use threads >70% of time.
- **Reactions/Emoji**: Upvotes show what's valuable. Top workflows get 20+ reactions; encourages more sharing.
- **Search**: Members ask in #help if question might've been answered before. Search returns past workflows.
- **Profiles**: Members fill out profiles with expertise (BuildFlow skills, background). Helps members find helpers.
- **Saved Messages**: Members save workflows to reference later. Generates engagement metrics.
- **Slack Connect**: Can invite guests from companies interested in BuildFlow; expands reach without spam.

Features Currently Missing (that would help compound):
- **Ranked/pinned workflows**: Top workflows get buried after 2 days. Would help if we had a "Featured workflows" channel.
- **Member reputation scoring**: No visible way to see who the most helpful people are. (Would encourage contribution.)
- **Workflow tagging**: Workflows are hard to find by use case. If members could tag "lead-gen" or "email-automation", discovery improves.

Platform Assessment:
- Chronological vs. engagement-weighted? Slack is chronological (less than ideal for compounding, but reactions help)
- Upvote/react feature? YES; heavy use (avg 3.2 reactions per post)
- Search? YES; indexed well; members use it
- Member profiles visible? YES; profiles encourage expertise signaling
- Recommendation algorithm? NO (Slack limitation); relying on human curation + search

---

COMMUNITY IMPACT ON BUSINESS METRICS

Community Cohort Analysis:
- Active community members (posted 1+ times): 337 members
- Retention (month 1): 94% (community members stay)
- Retention (month 3 projected): 88%
- Churn rate differential: Community members churn 8-12% less than non-members ✓

Recruitment & Referral Impact:
- % of new signups referred by community members: 23% (340 of 1,500 new signups in Feb-Mar)
- Referral volume per month: ~170 new signups from community
- Referral CAC vs. paid CAC: Referred users cost $0 to acquire vs. $45 average paid CAC ✓

Revenue Impact:
- LTV of community members: $340 (higher engagement; longer retention)
- LTV of non-members: $180 (lower engagement; higher churn)
- Uplift: Community members are 89% higher LTV ✓
- Upsell rate (free to paid tier) in community: 18% (vs. 9% non-members)

Community Cost:
- Monthly investment: $5,000 (1 FTE community manager $3k + platform/tools $1k + event costs $1k)
- ROI: (170 referrals × $340 LTV) / $5k = 11.6x first-month ROI ✓ (excellent)
- Payback period: 0.9 months (positive in month 1!)
- Long-term value: If community grows to 5,000 members, referral volume scales to 800+/month; ROI multiplies

---

GROWTH & REVITALIZATION PLAN

Current Status: Healthy and Compounding
- Growth exceeding targets in all dimensions
- Engagement depth strong (32% MAU, 28% posted)
- Member sentiment highly positive (4.3/5)
- No revival needed; focus on scaling

Optimization Opportunities (not revitalization):
1. **Add workflow tagging system** (by April 30)
   - Hypothesis: With tags, discovery improves; referrals from community increase by 15%
   - Success metric: Search usage increases 25%; new members find 3+ workflows in first week

2. **Create "Community Picks" curated workflow list** (by April 15)
   - Hypothesis: Featured list drives repeat visits; turns lurkers into engaged members
   - Success metric: 40% of new members click "Community Picks" in first week; 30% save at least one

3. **Recruit 3 new moderators from growing base** (by May 1)
   - Hypothesis: Distributed moderation scales community; moderators grow organically from community
   - Success metric: 3 new moderators recruited from members; moderation response time stays <45min

---

LAUNCH TIMELINE & CHECKLIST (Week 6 Status)

Month 1 (Feb): Foundation ✓
- [x] Assess community-market fit (High = validated)
- [x] Recruit 50 founding members (52 recruited)
- [x] Define culture & guidelines (Pinned, well-adopted)
- [x] Design member roles & escalation (Roles published; 5 moderators, 2 ambassadors identified)
- [x] Set up platform (Slack + workflows set up)

Month 2 (Mar): Growth & Programming ✓
- [x] Launch 8-week programming plan (7/8 events completed; attendance strong)
- [x] Promote members to Helpers/Builders (18 Helpers, 24 Builders identified and notified)
- [x] Share engagement metrics (weekly #metrics channel; 47% adoption)
- [x] Gather feedback on programming (Retrospective had 63 attendees; feedback shapes next phase)

Month 3 (Apr): Scaling & Optimization [IN PROGRESS]
- [x] Analyze engagement depth (Complete; 32% MAU, strong compounding signals)
- [x] Recruit moderators (5 moderators active; 3 more candidates identified)
- [x] Refine programming (Shifting to member-led; 40% of events now hosted by members)
- [ ] Plan phase 2 (Target: grow from 1.2k to 5k members by Q3; scale programming to 4-5 events/week)
```

## Edge Cases

- **Community-market fit is weak**: If your product doesn't naturally create peer value, a community will feel forced and fail. Better to skip it than build a dead one.

- **Community becomes too big too fast**: Slack communities >5k members need heavy moderation and multiple moderators. Growth pace matters more than size.

- **Members only lurk**: 90% lurking is normal. But if <10% engage, something's wrong with culture or programming. Fix that before growing.

- **Moderators burn out**: If you have 1 moderator managing 1k members, they'll quit. Distribute load across 4-5 people. Each person should handle <250 members max.

- **Community becomes sales channel**: If founder/team spends all time pitching product, members leave. Community is separate from sales; maintain culture.

- **Platform limits growth**: Slack is great for 1-2k members. Discord for 5k+. Forum for 10k+. Choose platform that matches growth trajectory.

items.name: Build Email Welcome Series
items.description: Design a complete email welcome series — 3 to 7 emails with timing, subject lines, content structure, and conversion goals. Build onboarding sequences, brand storytelling flows, or purchase-driving series. Trigger on: welcome series, welcome email, onboarding emails, welcome flow, new subscriber sequence, welcome automation.
items.author: Harmony

connectors.name: HubSpot
connectors.reason: Pull contact signup sources, lead scores, and engagement data to personalize welcome content and segment by user profile

connectors.name: Gmail
connectors.reason: Reference existing email templates and analyze past welcome email performance to validate subject lines and timing

connectors.name: Slack
connectors.reason: Surface team discussions about brand voice, value propositions, customer onboarding priorities, and known conversion blockers

connectors.name: Notion
connectors.reason: Reference brand guidelines, customer journey documentation, onboarding flows, and content calendars for consistency

items.prompt:
## Purpose

A welcome series is the first conversation with new subscribers. It sets expectations, establishes brand voice, builds trust, and drives the first action you need them to take. Unlike regular campaigns, a welcome series has a narrative arc: introduce yourself, prove value, address objections, and move to conversion. Done well, a welcome series converts 3–5x higher than standalone emails because subscribers are warmest in the first 48 hours. Done poorly, it overwhelms new users or pushes too hard too fast, creating unsubscribes before they understand who you are. This skill teaches you to build a series that respects the subscriber's journey, communicates your value clearly, and systematically moves them toward the action that matters most (signing up, booking a call, making a purchase, or activating a feature).

## When to Use

- **New product launch**: You have thousands of early access signups and need to activate them into trial users or paid customers
- **Growing a mailing list**: You've built organic or paid acquisition channels and now need to educate and convert those inbound leads
- **Re-launching a product or rebrand**: Existing subscribers need to understand what changed and why they should care
- **SaaS onboarding**: Free trial signups need a guided sequence to reach "aha moment" before they churn at day 7–14
- **eCommerce store launch**: You have email subscribers but no purchase history; welcome series educates them on your products and philosophy
- **Reactivating a list after long dormancy**: Your list got stale; a new welcome sequence re-introduces your value to people who signed up months ago
- **Testing a new product feature**: Show new users why the feature matters through a series of use case stories before asking them to try it
- **Building a customer community or membership**: New members need to understand the benefits, meet other members, and take first steps before they drop out

## Instructions

### 1. Define Your Welcome Series Goal

Clarify what success looks like before you write a single email. Are you trying to:
- **Activate** (get them to use your product/feature for the first time)
- **Educate** (help them understand your category or value proposition)
- **Convert** (move a free trial user to a paying customer)
- **Engage** (build relationship trust and open rates)
- **Refer** (get them to recommend you to others)

Most welcome series serve multiple goals, but rank them. Example: Canopy Health's goal is "Convert trial users to booking a first telehealth appointment" (primary) and "Build trust in our doctors" (secondary).

### 2. Map the Subscriber Journey

Understand who's subscribing and what they expect:
- **Signup source**: Did they click an ad, sign up on your website, attend a webinar, receive a referral? Each source brings different expectations.
- **First action you want them to take**: Not "open the email" — what's the *business* action? (Book a demo, start a trial, make a purchase, complete a profile, join a Slack group)
- **Perceived risk or objection**: What's stopping them from taking that action? Cost? Not understanding the problem? Skepticism of the solution? Uncertainty it will work for them?
- **Timeline to decision**: How long do they have before they decide to buy/churn/move on? (SaaS trial = 7–14 days, eCommerce = 3–7 days, B2B = 21–30 days)

Example: Canopy Health.
- Signup source: Paid search ("telehealth for primary care"), organic search ("urgent care online"), referral from existing patient
- First action: Book appointment within 7 days
- Perceived risk: "Are the doctors real doctors? Will they take my insurance? Will it be awkward on video?"
- Timeline: 7-day trial period before they churn

### 3. Design the Sequence: Timing and Structure

Use this as a template for 3–7 emails (5 emails is the sweet spot):

**Email 1: Welcome (0 hours – send immediately)**
- Goal: Confirm signup, set expectations, start building rapport
- Length: Short (100–150 words)
- CTA: Soft (confirm email, set up profile, or redirect to onboarding)
- Subject line approach: Personal + curiosity (e.g., "Welcome to Canopy — your first visit is free")
- Content: Thank them, explain what happens next, single clear instruction

**Email 2: Build Trust (6–24 hours later)**
- Goal: Establish credibility, answer immediate objection #1
- Length: Medium (200–300 words)
- CTA: Moderate (book appointment, start trial, read comparison)
- Subject line approach: Benefit or social proof (e.g., "How 50K patients skip the waiting room")
- Content: Tell a story or share social proof. Prove you understand their problem. Mention specific customer wins or credentials.

**Email 3: Add Value (2–3 days later)**
- Goal: Give something useful, prove you're not just selling
- Length: Medium (200–300 words)
- CTA: Moderate (download guide, watch demo, join community)
- Subject line approach: Curiosity or utility (e.g., "The checklist telehealth doctors use to avoid misdiagnosis")
- Content: Guide, checklist, case study, or resource. Show your expertise. This email often gets highest engagement because it's truly valuable, not sales-y.

**Email 4: Address Objection #2 and Create Urgency (5–7 days later)**
- Goal: Address the second most common objection, create gentle urgency
- Length: Medium (250–350 words)
- CTA: Direct (book now, start trial, apply discount)
- Subject line approach: Specific benefit or scarcity (e.g., "Why most people wait too long to book (and regret it)" or "48-hour bonus: First visit completely free")
- Content: Show what happens if they don't act (opportunity cost), share testimonial about how customer wished they started sooner, or explain limited-time offer

**Email 5: Final Conversion Ask (9–11 days later)**
- Goal: Move those still undecided to action
- Length: Medium (250–350 words)
- CTA: Direct (book, buy, start trial)
- Subject line approach: Urgency or personal (e.g., "Last chance — your 7-day free trial ends soon" or "Sarah — are you still interested in that free visit?")
- Content: Remind them what they'll get, address final objections, remove friction (show exact next steps, payment is simple, etc.). Include a reply CTA ("Just hit reply and let me know if you have questions").

**Email 6 (Optional): Last-Ditch Conversion (12–14 days later, if Email 5 didn't convert)**
- Goal: Recover those about to churn from trial
- Length: Short and personal (150–200 words)
- CTA: Direct (final offer)
- Subject line approach: Personal curiosity (e.g., "One more thing about Canopy...")
- Content: Very personal tone. "I noticed you haven't booked an appointment yet. I'm worried we're not a good fit, or you have questions we haven't answered. Hit reply — I want to help." This feels human, not sales-y.

**Email 7 (Optional): Sunset (15–21 days later, if Email 6 didn't convert)**
- Goal: Let them go gracefully, leave the door open
- Length: Short (100–150 words)
- CTA: Soft ("Come back anytime")
- Subject line approach: Respectful (e.g., "We'll be here when you're ready")
- Content: "We get it — timing might not be right. No pressure. If things change and you want to give Canopy a try, just click below or hit reply. We're here."

### 4. Write Subject Lines Using Proven Frameworks

Subject lines drive opens; opens drive conversions. Use these frameworks:

- **Curiosity + specificity**: "Why 50K patients skip the waiting room" (better than "Try Canopy," which is vague)
- **Benefit + proof**: "Book your appointment in 90 seconds — no insurance forms" (tangible time/effort savings)
- **Personal + context**: "Sarah, your free visit expires in 2 days" (name + urgency)
- **Question + hook**: "What if your doctor visit lasted 5 minutes instead of 45?" (invites thought)
- **Social proof + emotional**: "See why Dr. Chen patients give 4.9 stars" (credibility + curiosity)
- **Urgency + clarity**: "48-hour bonus: First visit completely free" (deadline + specific offer)

Avoid generic openers: "Check this out," "FYI," "Update," "Reminder." They blend into 100 other emails.

### 5. Structure Each Email: Hook, Value, CTA

Every email follows this pattern:
1. **Hook** (first sentence, subject line decides if they open; first sentence decides if they read): Start with curiosity, relevance, or surprise. "Most people wait 2–3 weeks for a doctor appointment. You can get one in 6 hours." Not "Hi there! We're excited to introduce Canopy."
2. **Value delivery** (body): Tell a story, share social proof, give a resource, or answer an objection. Make them feel seen and understood.
3. **Single CTA** (close): One button or link. "Book your free appointment" or "Watch the 2-minute demo." Multiple CTAs dilute action.

Example Email 2 from Canopy:

Subject: How 50K patients skip the waiting room

Hi Sarah,

Most people think telehealth means lower quality care. Shorter appointments. Nurses instead of doctors.

It's not.

At Canopy, every patient sees a board-certified MD. Full 20-30 minute visit, not rushed. And yes—we accept your insurance (we checked).

50,000 patients have switched from waiting 3 weeks for an appointment to seeing a doctor in 6 hours. Here's what they say:

"I was skeptical at first. The visit was thorough. Dr. Chen spent more time with me than my in-person doctor ever did." — Maria S.

"I needed antibiotics and didn't want to sit in an urgent care for 2 hours. Done in 30 minutes from my kitchen." — James K.

It's the same care you'd get in-person. Just without the waiting room.

Ready to try it?

[BUTTON: Book Your Free Visit]

Not sure yet? Check out our doc profiles—see who you might see if you book.

---

### 6. Set Conversion Goals Per Email

Define what success looks like for each email:

- **Email 1**: 15–25% open rate (very warm). If below 10%, subject line is weak.
- **Email 2**: 10–15% open rate (still warm). 2–3% click rate minimum. If clicks are low, social proof isn't resonating.
- **Email 3**: 12–18% open rate (this email often outperforms Email 2 because it's valuable, not sales-y). 3–5% click rate.
- **Email 4**: 10–15% open rate. 3–4% click-to-conversion rate (some readers take action; this email closes deals).
- **Email 5**: 8–12% open rate. 4–6% click-to-conversion rate (final push).

Set these targets before you launch. After the series runs, compare actual to target. If Email 2 opens are 5%, the subject line needs rewriting. If Email 3 clicks are 1%, the value isn't clear enough.

### 7. Add Conditional Logic and Branches

Simple version: Send the same series to everyone.

Better version: Branch based on action.

Example:
- If they click "Book appointment" in Email 2, skip Email 3 (they're moving to conversion) and send a "appointment confirmed" email instead.
- If they open Email 1 but don't open Email 2, resend Email 2 with a different subject line.
- If they open Email 2 but don't click, send a version of Email 3 with more social proof (they're skeptical, not uninterested).
- If they don't convert by Email 5, segment them: "high-intent" (clicked but didn't book) get Email 6 with a discount. "Low-intent" (no clicks) get sunset Email 7.

This requires automation platform logic (HubSpot workflows, Klaviyo flows, ConvertKit segments). Start simple, add complexity as you learn what works.

### 8. Define Success Metrics and Optimization Plan

Track:
- **Series conversion rate**: % of people who enter the series that complete the goal action (book, buy, sign up for trial). Target: 5–15% depending on product/market.
- **Per-email metrics**: Open rate, click rate, unsubscribe rate. Use these to identify weak emails.
- **Time to conversion**: How long between first email and goal action? Faster is better (means your series is persuasive and well-timed).
- **CTA performance**: Which CTAs get most clicks? Use the winner in future series.
- **Unsubscribe rate**: If >0.5% on a single email, that email feels sales-y or pushy. Rewrite it to be more value-focused.

Example metrics from Canopy:
- Series conversion rate: 23% (from welcome series to booked appointment)
- Email 1 open: 68%, click: 12%
- Email 2 open: 42%, click: 8% (this email builds trust; it's effective even with lower open rate)
- Email 3 open: 38%, click: 11% (value-driven email; high click despite lower opens)
- Email 4 open: 31%, click: 14% (urgency works; this email drives most conversions)
- Email 5 open: 24%, click: 9% (fatigue sets in by email 5; many converted already)

Optimization: Email 2 has high abandonment (42% open, but only 8% click). A/B test social proof: Does "50K patients" or "4.9-star rating from 12K reviews" work better?

### 9. Deliver the Complete Series as a Structured Playbook

Document the series in a format your team can implement:

```
WELCOME SERIES PLAYBOOK: [Product Name]

Goal: [What action do we want subscribers to take?]
Target conversion rate: [X%]
Timeline to conversion: [X days]

SERIES OVERVIEW
Email 1: [Subject line]
Email 2: [Subject line]
Email 3: [Subject line]
Email 4: [Subject line]
Email 5: [Subject line]

[Full email copies for each]

CONDITIONAL BRANCHES
If [action], then [send this email or adjust timing]

METRICS TO TRACK
[Targets for open rate, click rate, conversion rate, per email]

OPTIMIZATION PLAN
Month 1: Launch and collect baseline metrics
Month 2: A/B test subject lines on Email 2 (highest objection) and Email 4 (highest conversion driver)
Month 3: Test conditional branches (skip email if already converted)
```

## Output Format

```
=== WELCOME SERIES PLAYBOOK ===

PRODUCT / COMPANY: [Name]

GOAL AND TIMELINE
Primary goal: [What action do we want? Book appointment, make first purchase, start free trial?]
Target subscriber: [Who is signing up? Where do they come from?]
Days to conversion: [X]
Target conversion rate: [X%]

SUBSCRIBER JOURNEY
Signup source: [Where do they come from?]
First action desired: [Not "open email" — what's the business outcome?]
Key objection: [What's stopping them?]
Timeline to decision: [How long before they decide yes/no?]

SERIES STRUCTURE
Email 1: [Send immediately]
  Subject: [Subject line]
  Goal: [Confirm, set expectations, soft CTA]
  CTA: [Specific action button]

Email 2: [Send 24 hours later]
  Subject: [Subject line]
  Goal: [Build trust, prove credibility]
  CTA: [Specific action button]

[... repeat for emails 3–7]

FULL EMAIL COPY
[For each email, include:
- Subject line
- Full body copy
- CTA button text]

CONDITIONAL LOGIC
If [behavior], then [send this variation or skip email]

METRICS AND TARGETS
Email 1: [X% open rate target], [X% click rate target]
Email 2: [X% open rate target], [X% click rate target]
Series: [X% conversion rate target]

OPTIMIZATION PLAN
Month 1: [What to test]
Month 2: [What to test]
```

## Example

**Company: Canopy Health (telehealth startup, 12K new signups/month)**

```
=== WELCOME SERIES PLAYBOOK ===

PRODUCT: Canopy Health (telehealth appointments)

GOAL AND TIMELINE
Primary goal: Get subscriber to book their first telehealth appointment with a doctor
Target subscriber: People searching "telehealth near me" or "urgent care online" (paid search), + word-of-mouth referrals from existing patients
Days to conversion: 7 days (free trial period)
Target conversion rate: 20% (our baseline; stretch goal is 25%)

SUBSCRIBER JOURNEY
Signup source: 65% paid search (Google Ads: "telehealth," "urgent care," "same-day doctor"), 20% organic search, 15% referral from existing patient
First action desired: Book appointment within 7 days of signup (signals serious intent, prevents churn at trial expiration)
Key objection: "Are these real doctors? Will they be thorough? Will my insurance cover it? Will it be awkward on video?"
Timeline to decision: 7 days (trial expiration triggers auto-unsubscribe if no appointment booked)

SERIES STRUCTURE

Email 1: Confirm Signup (Send immediately, 0 hours)
  Subject: Welcome to Canopy — your first visit is free
  Goal: Confirm email, set expectations, start building rapport
  CTA: "Set Up Your Profile" (this is a soft first action that signals commitment without requiring a booking yet)

Email 2: Build Trust (Send 24 hours later)
  Subject: Why 50K patients skip the waiting room
  Goal: Establish credibility, share social proof, answer objection about doctor quality
  CTA: "See Our Doctors" or "Book Your Appointment"

Email 3: Add Value (Send 3 days after signup)
  Subject: The doctor's checklist for telehealth visits (download free)
  Goal: Give something useful (educational resource), prove expertise, reduce friction
  CTA: "Download Checklist"

Email 4: Create Urgency (Send 5 days after signup)
  Subject: Your free visit expires in 2 days
  Goal: Address second objection (insurance/cost), create time urgency, offer specific reassurance
  CTA: "Book Now"

Email 5: Final Ask (Send 6.5 days after signup, morning before trial expires)
  Subject: Sarah — your free visit is available through tonight
  Goal: Move undecided to action, remove final friction (show exact next steps, payment is easy)
  CTA: "Book Your Appointment"

FULL EMAIL COPY

--- EMAIL 1 ---
Subject: Welcome to Canopy — your first visit is free

Hi Sarah,

Thanks for signing up. You're in.

Your first visit is completely free. Here's what happens next:

1. Confirm your email (click the button below)
2. Set up your profile (2 minutes: name, age, insurance, what you need a doctor for)
3. Pick a doctor and book an appointment—available today or anytime this week

If you have questions, just hit reply. I'm here.

[BUTTON: Confirm Email & Set Up Profile]

See you soon,
The Canopy team

---

--- EMAIL 2 ---
Subject: Why 50K patients skip the waiting room

Hi Sarah,

I'm going to be honest: most people think telehealth means lower quality care. Shorter appointments. Nurses instead of doctors. Second-rate service.

We hear it all the time.

It's not true.

At Canopy, every patient sees a board-certified MD—not a nurse practitioner. You get a full 20–30 minute appointment, not a rushed 5-minute chat. And yes, we accept your insurance (I checked the common ones; let us know yours).

50,000+ patients have made the switch from waiting 3 weeks for an appointment to getting one with a real doctor in 6 hours. Here's what they're saying:

"I was skeptical about telehealth. My visit was thorough. Dr. Chen actually spent more time with me than my in-person doctor. I'm a believer now." — Maria S., Brooklyn

"I needed antibiotics and didn't want to sit in an urgent care for 2 hours. I was on video with a doctor in 30 minutes. Done." — James K., Austin

"It's the same care you'd get walking into a clinic. The only difference is you don't sit in a waiting room for 45 minutes breathing in everyone's colds." — Alex T., San Francisco

That's because they ARE the same doctors. Board-certified. Licensed. Real.

Ready to see what the hype is about?

[BUTTON: Book Your Appointment]

Or if you want to know more about who you'd see, check out our doctors below. Read their bios, see their ratings, pick someone you trust.

See you soon,
The Canopy team

P.S. — Your first visit is free. Seriously. No credit card required, no surprise charges.

---

--- EMAIL 3 ---
Subject: The doctor's checklist for telehealth visits (download)

Hi Sarah,

Here's a download we're sharing with all our new patients: a checklist from our doctors about how to get the most out of a telehealth visit.

It's quick (one page). It covers:
- What to have handy before your visit (insurance card, list of current medications, phone camera that works)
- The 3 questions our doctors wish every patient asked (these catch things that get missed)
- Why video visits are actually better than in-person for certain situations (you're in a familiar environment, less nervous, better communication)
- What to do if the tech doesn't work (spoiler: we have a backup phone line)

[LINK: Download "How to Get the Most Out of Your Telehealth Visit"]

This document came from real doctor feedback. Read it, and when you book your visit, you'll feel more prepared.

See you soon,
The Canopy team

---

--- EMAIL 4 ---
Subject: Your free visit expires in 2 days — here's what I need you to know

Hi Sarah,

I want to flag something: your trial access expires in 2 days. Once it expires, your first visit won't be free anymore (it'll be $89, covered by insurance if you have it).

Here's the thing though: I noticed you haven't booked yet. I'm not sure if it's:
- You're waiting for the right moment
- You have a question we haven't answered
- You're not sure what you'd even talk to a doctor about (spoiler: you don't need a specific "problem"—routine checkups, medication refills, second opinions all count)
- You're worried about insurance coverage (we cover 95% of plans; you can check yours in 30 seconds)
- You're skeptical it's actually quality care (fair; that's why we share our doctor bios and 12K+ patient reviews)

Hit reply and tell me what's holding you back. Seriously. If we're not a fit, I'll tell you. If we are, I'll help you book in 60 seconds.

Or just book here:

[BUTTON: Book Your Free Visit Before It Expires]

Your call. But the free trial advantage is real—use it while you've got it.

See you soon,
Sarah's Canopy guide

---

--- EMAIL 5 ---
Subject: Sarah — your free visit is available through tonight

Hi Sarah,

Last check in: your free first visit is available through 11:59 PM tonight. After that, it's $89 (or whatever your insurance covers).

Here's the fast path to booking:

1. Click the button below
2. Pick a time today or tomorrow (same-day appointments often available)
3. You're done. Doctor will call you at your appointment time.

No forms. No waiting room. No surprises.

[BUTTON: Book Your Appointment Now]

If you want to see doctor options first, go here: [link to doctor profiles]

One more thing: if the cost is a concern, let me know. We have payment plans and hardship options.

Hope to see you on a call soon,
The Canopy team

---

CONDITIONAL BRANCHES

IF subscriber clicks "Book Your Appointment" in Email 2 or Email 4:
THEN skip remaining welcome emails, send "appointment confirmed" email instead with:
- Appointment time, doctor name, how to join
- What to prepare
- How to cancel if needed
- Link to doctor profile

IF subscriber opens Email 1 but doesn't open Email 2:
THEN resend Email 2 with subject line variation ("4.9-star rating from 12K real patients" instead of "50K patients")

IF subscriber opens Email 2 but doesn't click:
THEN send modified Email 3 with more social proof (testimonials instead of checklist download)

IF subscriber doesn't convert by Email 5:
THEN segment into two paths:
- Path A (High intent: opened 4+ emails): Send Email 6 with "$20 off your first visit if you book this week"
- Path B (Low intent: opened 0–2 emails): Send sunset Email 7 "we'll be here when you're ready"

METRICS AND TARGETS

Email 1: 65% open rate, 12% click-through rate
Email 2: 40% open rate, 8% click-through rate
Email 3: 38% open rate, 9% click-through rate
Email 4: 32% open rate, 14% click-through rate
Email 5: 25% open rate, 11% click-through rate
Series conversion rate: 20%
Average days to conversion: 3.5 days (faster is better; indicates urgency and relevance)
Unsubscribe rate: <0.3% per email (high unsubscribes = email is pushy or irrelevant)

OPTIMIZATION PLAN

Month 1: Launch full series. Collect baseline metrics. Identify lowest-performing email by click rate.
Month 2: A/B test Email 2 and Email 4 subject lines. Email 2 builds trust (test social proof angles); Email 4 drives conversions (test urgency vs. objection-handling).
Month 3: Implement conditional branches (skip email if already converted). Test split between "high intent" and "low intent" paths.
Month 4: Test Email 3 format (checklist vs. video vs. testimonial) based on engagement data.
```

## Edge Cases

- **Mixed signup sources with different expectations**: Paid search signups (urgent need) expect faster conversion path than organic search (research phase). Consider separate welcome series for high-intent vs. research-phase subscribers, or add branching logic that moves high-intent users faster to conversion CTA.

- **Multiple conversion goals in one series**: If you're trying to drive both "start trial" AND "upgrade to annual billing," you confuse subscribers and dilute conversions. Pick one primary goal. If secondary goal is important, create a separate series that runs after the primary goal is achieved.

- **Long sales cycles (B2B)**: SaaS sales cycles are 7–14 days; B2B enterprise is 30–90 days. A 5-email series that runs out by day 7 doesn't work. Extend to 8–12 emails with longer gaps, or transition to a nurture series that runs in parallel.

- **High unsubscribe rate on a specific email**: If Email 4 has 1.2% unsubscribe rate while others are 0.2%, that email is too sales-y or doesn't fit the narrative. It's breaking trust. Rewrite it to add value before asking for the conversion.

- **Conversion happens before series ends**: Great problem. Use conditional logic to skip remaining emails when someone converts. Otherwise, they get Email 5 asking them to book an appointment they already booked, which looks broken.

- **Welcome series conflicts with re-engagement or nurture campaigns**: If someone already gets a separate "re-engagement" sequence, don't also send them the welcome series. Set suppression rules so they only get one series at a time.

- **Testing fatigue**: Don't A/B test every email in a 5-email series simultaneously. You'll have statistically invalid results because sample sizes get small. Test one email (the one with lowest performance) at a time. Let it run for one full cycle before moving to the next.

- **Measuring conversion requires multi-touch attribution**: If someone opens Email 2 but books 2 days later without clicking the Email 2 link, did Email 2 drive the conversion? Yes, it built trust. Your email tool might not track this. Use UTM parameters on every CTA link to track which email drove the click, but acknowledge that earlier emails in the series also contributed.

items.name: Build Platform Strategy
items.description: Create a platform strategy that enables third-party developers to build on top of your product. When evaluating whether to stay vertical or open an ecosystem. Trigger on: developer experience, ecosystem, API strategy, third-party integration, marketplace, platform launch, governance.
items.author: Harmony

connectors.name: Linear
connectors.reason: Tracks platform development roadmap, API features, developer feedback

connectors.name: Slack
connectors.reason: Developer community channel for feedback, support, and ecosystem coordination

connectors.name: Notion
