items.prompt:
## Purpose

A platform transforms your product from a closed system into an ecosystem where developers build on top of it, expanding capability without increasing your team. Platforms create network effects: more developers → more apps → more users → more developers. The shift from product to platform is not about adding APIs and calling it done. It requires rethinking everything: governance (what can third parties do?), developer experience (how fast can someone build?), economics (how do you capture value?), and go-to-market (who are your developer champions?). This skill guides you through deciding whether a platform is right for your company, building it in phases, and measuring ecosystem health.

## When to Use This

- You've achieved strong product-market fit but growth is plateauing despite adding features
- Customers repeatedly ask for integrations or custom extensions you can't build alone
- You're seeing users build workarounds or middleware; opportunity cost of building is clear
- Your roadmap is constrained by trying to serve every vertical use case
- You want to compete with larger platforms without building everything yourself
- You're losing deals to competitors with richer ecosystems
- Your product is mature enough to be stable; developer-facing features won't destabilize core
- You have the engineering resources to support a public API and developer community
- You're comfortable trading some control for faster innovation at the edges

## Instructions

1. **Evaluate Platform Timing and Market Fit**
   - Assess your product maturity: is core product stable enough for external developers? (target: <1% daily API failures, documented patterns)
   - Map customer requests: are 20%+ of inbound asks about integrations, plugins, or custom extensions?
   - Research adjacent pain points: which use cases are outside your roadmap but highly requested?
   - Analyze competitor ecosystems: how many apps does your closest competitor have? How active is their marketplace?
   - Calculate internal capacity: how many engineers would it take to build the top 10 customer requests in-house? (if >3 FTE, platform economics favor opening)
   - Talk to 20 customers about a hypothetical platform: would they build on it? What integrations matter most?
   - Decision framework: if you can honestly say "we don't have enough engineers to serve all use cases," a platform is likely right

2. **Define Platform Boundaries and Governance**
   - Decide what developers CAN build: Can they access user data? Modify core workflows? Create revenue-sharing deals?
   - Create a tier system: basic integrations (webhooks, read-only APIs) vs. advanced (full data access, custom UI in your app)
   - Write down what developers CANNOT do: no competing product category, no scraping, no reverse-engineering, no spam
   - Design revenue share (if applicable): 70/30, 80/20, or free tier with premium revenue share?
   - Specify enforcement: how do you manage apps that break rules? Removal process? Appeal process?
   - Document security requirements: data encryption, auth standards, API rate limits, audit logging
   - Create a simple one-page platform policy; clarity prevents disputes

3. **Build Developer Experience as Your Product**
   - Create API documentation that enables a developer to build an app in <2 hours without contacting support
   - Provide SDKs in the languages your users code in (if you're a design tool, JavaScript; if a backend service, Python/Go)
   - Build a sandbox environment where developers test without hitting production
   - Create a developer console dashboard where devs manage API keys, see usage, debug errors in real-time
   - Write 5-10 example integrations (Slack notifications, Google Sheets sync, CRM import) to show what's possible
   - Set up a 20-minute onboarding call template; some developers need human help despite great docs
   - Measure time-to-first-API-call: target <30 minutes from signup to working code
   - Create a support channel: dedicated Slack community, Discord server, or email alias for developer questions

4. **Launch a Minimal Viable Ecosystem**
   - Start with read-only APIs and webhooks; avoid complex state-changing operations initially
   - Open to 50-100 trusted developers first (beta); avoid public launch until you've solved governance edge cases
   - Identify 5 "seed" developers (customers who've built workarounds) and co-develop apps with them
   - Create a lightweight app marketplace or directory (even a simple table on your website)
   - Recruit 2-3 champion developers who become your advocates; make them feel like co-creators
   - Measure: days-to-first-app, number of developers building, volume of API calls
   - If you hit 20 developers, 5 published apps, 1M+ API calls/month, you're ready to expand

5. **Build and Maintain Developer Community**
   - Create a weekly developer office hours (30 min, open Q&A) to build relationships
   - Publish a monthly developer newsletter: new API features, featured apps, community wins
   - Highlight top developers: feature them in your blog, give them free credits, invite to customer advisory board
   - Create a developer Slack community where devs help each other (reduces support burden)
   - Host an annual or quarterly developer conference (even virtual); make it a big deal
   - Solicit feature requests formally: "What API feature would unlock your next app?"
   - Track sentiment: are developers excited or frustrated? Survey them quarterly

6. **Design the Marketplace (When Ready)**
   - Decide: centralized marketplace (you vet apps) or open app store (anyone publishes)?
   - Create app listing standard: name, description, logo, pricing (free/paid), permissions required, user reviews
   - Build review process: security check, user experience check, data privacy audit
   - Implement app ratings and reviews: social proof drives adoption
   - Enable revenue sharing: if an app charges, developers get 70%, you get 30%
   - Ensure fair app discovery: featured apps, category browse, search, trending list
   - Set growth targets: 100 published apps in year one, 50+ active developers
   - Plan marketplace moderation: remove broken apps, spam, or malicious integrations quickly

7. **Measure Platform Health and Growth**
   - **Developer Health:** Active developers (monthly), app published count, time-to-first-app
   - **Usage Health:** API calls/month, webhook events/month, average dev quota usage (% of limit)
   - **Ecosystem Contribution:** Apps that reduce support tickets, customer churn prevented by key integrations
   - **Revenue Impact:** Third-party-influenced ARR (customers who cite app use as stickiness factor), revenue from partner revenue share
   - **Satisfaction:** Developer NPS, support resolution time, feature request velocity
   - Create a monthly platform scorecard; share with exec team and product team
   - If API calls are stagnant for 2 months, investigate: Are docs unclear? Did an app break? Do devs need new features?
   - Benchmark against competitors: how many developers, apps, and API calls do they have? Healthy platforms grow 30%+ YoY

8. **Avoid Platform Pitfalls**
   - Don't compete with your ecosystem: if a top developer builds an app, don't build the same thing and push it as "official"
   - Don't change API contracts without warning: versioning matters; keep old versions alive for >1 year
   - Don't neglect security: one data breach or hacked app damages trust in the whole platform
   - Don't promise marketplace revenue if you can't deliver: transparent economics beat optimistic projections
   - Don't ignore toxic developers: one bad actor ruins community morale

## Output Format

```
## Platform Strategy Review

**Review Date:** [Month/Year]

### Market Context
- Product maturity level: [e.g., 3+ years, stable core, <1% API error rate]
- Customer demand signal: [X]% of inbound requests are integration/extension asks
- Competitor ecosystem size: [Competitor] has [X] apps, [X] developers
- Internal resource constraint: Would take [X] FTE to build top 10 customer requests in-house

### Strategic Decision
- Platform status: [Considering / Early Stage / Beta / Public / Mature]
- Platform thesis: "We believe [X] developers will build integrations that solve [Y use cases] we don't have time for"
- Go-to-market timeline: [e.g., "Beta launch with 50 developers in Q2, public marketplace in Q3"]

### Governance Framework
- Data access tier: [Read-only, read-write, custom data fields]
- Revenue share: [e.g., "Free tier: 70/30 split; Premium tier: 80/20 split"]
- Enforcement process: [Quick summary of how broken apps are handled]
- Security requirements: [e.g., OAuth2, data encryption, rate limits]

### Developer Experience Status
| Metric | Target | Current | Status |
|--------|--------|---------|--------|
| Time-to-first-API-call | <30 min | [X] min | ✓/✗ |
| Documentation coverage | 100% | [X]% | ✓/✗ |
| SDK availability | Python, JS, Go | [List] | ✓/✗ |
| Support response time | <24h | [X]h | ✓/✗ |
| Sandbox environment | Available | Yes/No | ✓/✗ |

### Ecosystem Health
- Active developers (monthly): [X] (trend: ↑/↓)
- Published apps: [X] (trend: ↑/↓)
- API calls/month: [X] (trend: ↑/↓)
- Developer NPS: [X] (target: >50)
- Churn rate: [X]% of developers per month

### Featured Integrations
1. **[App Name]** — [Use case], [X] users, [Impact]
2. **[App Name]** — [Use case], [X] users, [Impact]
3. **[App Name]** — [Use case], [X] users, [Impact]

### Community Activity
- Slack community members: [X]
- Support tickets/month: [X]
- Feature requests received: [X]
- Developer satisfaction: [Quote or sentiment]

### Business Impact (if applicable)
- Revenue from ecosystem: [X]
- Customers citing integrations as stickiness factor: [X]%
- Churn prevention: [X] customer accounts retained due to key integrations

### Roadmap (Next 90 Days)
- Developer experience: [e.g., "Launch Python SDK", "Improve webhook documentation"]
- Governance: [e.g., "Finalize revenue share model"]
- Ecosystem growth: [e.g., "Recruit 20 more beta developers"]
- Community: [e.g., "Launch developer office hours"]

### Risks and Mitigations
| Risk | Probability | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|-------------|--------|-----------|
| [e.g., "App quality issues damage trust"] | High | High | [e.g., "Implement app review process"] |
| [e.g., "Developers can't find quality docs"] | Medium | High | [e.g., "Hire docs engineer"] |
| [e.g., "Marketplace revenue splits don't attract top devs"] | Medium | Medium | [e.g., "Provide co-marketing to top developers"] |
```

## Example

**Company:** BuildSync (project management platform for construction)

**Scenario:** BuildSync launched in 2020 as a vertical-specific project management tool. By 2024, they had 8,000 paying customers but growth was slowing. Customers repeatedly asked for QuickBooks integration, Slack notifications, and custom field types. BuildSync was considering a platform strategy.

### Market Evaluation

BuildSync surveyed 30 customers: 73% said integrations would make them less likely to switch to Procore or Monday.com. BuildSync's engineering team estimated 4-6 FTE for the top 10 integration requests over the next year.

Building the platform made economic sense: 4-6 FTE ≈ $600K-$900K in annual cost. An ecosystem of developers building integrations could deliver the same value at a fraction of the cost, with faster time-to-value.

### Phase 1: Beta (Q2 2024)

BuildSync designed a minimal governance framework:
- Read-only APIs (project data, task status, team members)
- Webhook events (task created, project updated)
- OAuth 2.0 authentication
- Revenue share: 70 developer / 30 BuildSync (if developers charge)
- Policy: no competing products, no data scraping, no spam

They documented the API (took 3 weeks), built a sandbox, and recruited 30 beta developers from their customer base.

Key integrations launched:
- **QuickBooks Sync** (built by a construction accountant) — import hours worked, push to payroll
- **Slack Notifications** (built by a BuildSync customer) — real-time task updates to Slack
- **Custom Fields** (built by BuildSync internally as a sample) — show how to extend data model

After 8 weeks: 25 developers actively building, 3 published apps, 800K+ API calls/month. Developer NPS: 47 (not great, but improving).

### Phase 2: Improvements (Q3 2024)

BuildSync addressed developer feedback:
- "API docs need examples" → Added 20 Postman examples
- "Webhook retries are failing" → Improved webhook reliability (went from 97% to 99.9% delivery)
- "Hard to debug in production" → Built a webhook replay tool in developer console
- "How do I monetize?" → Simplified revenue share setup

### Phase 3: Public Launch (Q4 2024)

BuildSync launched a public app marketplace. Criteria for publishing:
- App must pass security review (OAuth, data encryption)
- Must solve a real problem (e.g., "integrate with tool X")
- Must have documentation and support contact
- Rating system: other BuildSync users rate integrations

First 90 days of public marketplace:
- 12 new apps published
- 150+ new developers signed up
- 5M+ API calls/month (6x growth from beta)
- Developer NPS improved to 62
- 2 apps earning >$10K/month in revenue (80/20 split)
- 300 paying customers reporting "integrations influenced our renewal" in surveys

### Key Learnings

- **Developer-first mentality pays off:** When BuildSync prioritized developer experience (docs, sandbox, office hours), NPS and engagement jumped
- **Seed developers are co-creators:** The accountant who built QuickBooks Sync became BuildSync's top evangelist; they featured her in case studies and product updates
- **Revenue share is credibility:** The first apps earning real money proved developers could build viable businesses on BuildSync, attracting higher-quality developers
- **Ecosystem compounds growth:** Each new app made BuildSync more "sticky" and reduced churn; customers on 2+ integrations had 95% annual retention vs. 72% for single-product users

---

## Edge Cases

1. **Platform Too Early (No Product-Market Fit):** If your core product isn't stable or customers are churning for core feature gaps, a platform wastes resources. Fix the core product first. Platforms are for companies past the PMF phase, not those still searching.

2. **Ecosystem Cannibalization:** If a top developer builds a competing feature (e.g., advanced reporting) that outshines yours, you face a choice: kill the app (erode trust) or compete fairly (dilutes focus). Governance frameworks help prevent this, but some friction is inevitable. Plan for it; don't be caught off-guard.

3. **Low Developer Adoption:** If 6 months in you have <20 active developers, the platform may not be the right motion. Reasons: unfamiliar API patterns, missing SDKs, or simply that developers prefer your product to be closed and simple. Pivot to narrow integrations (pre-built only) rather than open platform.

4. **Quality Control at Scale:** As your marketplace grows to 100+ apps, maintaining quality becomes expensive. One malicious or broken app damages trust in the entire ecosystem. Invest in automated security scanning, user reviews, and quick removal processes.

5. **Revenue Models That Don't Work:** If marketplace apps don't generate revenue and you can't subsidize developers forever, growth stalls. Consider: free tools with premium support, freemium models where developers upgrade for higher rate limits, or co-marketing partnerships.

6. **Developer Churn:** If developers stop building after their first app, you've failed. Reasons: low user demand, unclear monetization, better platforms elsewhere. Track why developers churn and re-engage top performers with new opportunities.

items.name: Build PMM Strategy
items.description: Create a product marketing strategy — positioning, messaging, competitive differentiation, and sales enablement — grounded in customer voice from sales calls and product feedback sessions. Trigger on: product marketing strategy, PMM plan, positioning framework, messaging house, competitive messaging, sales enablement content, product narrative, go-to-market messaging.
items.author: Harmony
items.prompt:
## Purpose

Product marketing managers live at the intersection of product, sales, and marketing — and the best ones know that their strategy must be grounded in customer and prospect voice, not internal assumptions. This skill mines Harmony transcripts (discovery calls, objection patterns, win/loss interviews, product feedback sessions) to extract the exact language customers use to describe their problems, your product, and competitive alternatives. From those insights, it builds a coherent positioning framework, messaging hierarchy, competitive narratives, and sales enablement materials that sales teams actually use and trust.

## When to Use

- **New product positioning**: Launching a new product or major feature and need to define how it fits in the market
- **Competitive response**: Responding to a new competitor or market entrant with differentiated messaging
- **Sales struggling with messaging**: Deal velocity is slow, and reps are confusing prospects with inconsistent value propositions
- **Analyst briefing prep**: Preparing to brief analysts or press and need a clear, defensible narrative
- **Quarterly messaging refresh**: Quarterly business reviews or planning cycles require updated positioning based on recent customer feedback
- **Product-market fit validation**: Validating that your core value proposition resonates by testing it against customer objections and win/loss data

## Instructions

**Step 1: Mine Customer Voice**
Review Harmony transcripts from discovery calls, product feedback sessions, and win/loss interviews. Extract:
- Exact language customers use to describe their problem (not your solution)
- How prospects describe competing solutions or status quo
- Objections and concerns that appear repeatedly
- Words and phrases that signal resonance (customers repeat them back unprompted)
- Proof points and success metrics customers mention

**Step 2: Build Positioning Canvas**
From customer voice, define:
- **Target Segment**: Who are we solving for? (job title, company size, use case, decision driver)
- **Category**: How do customers naturally bucket what you do?
- **Key Differentiator**: What is the one thing about us that matters most to this segment?
- **Proof Points**: Customer evidence (names, metrics, outcomes) that supports the differentiator

**Step 3: Create Messaging Hierarchy**
Build a three-tier structure:
- **Headline**: One sentence that captures the core value (customer problem → unique solution)
- **Value Pillars** (3–4): The main reasons to believe, each grounded in customer evidence
- **Proof Points & Handlers**: Stories, metrics, customer quotes, and objection handlers for each pillar

**Step 4: Map Competitive Narrative**
For each major competitor:
- How do customers and prospects describe them?
- What are they good at (and what do we cede)?
- What is our strongest angle against them?
- What messaging positions us as the clear alternative?

**Step 5: Design Sales Enablement Assets**
Create materials sales teams will actually use:
- **One-pagers**: Problem → solution → proof for each segment
- **Battlecards**: Competitor comparisons, objection handlers, win strategies
- **Email templates**: Discovery, objection response, follow-up hooks
- **Talk tracks**: How to open a call, handle discovery questions, close on next steps

**Step 6: Define Personas with Conversation Evidence**
For each persona (e.g., VP of Sales, Marketing Operations Manager):
- Job title, company size, key challenges
- How they talk about the problem
- What success looks like to them
- Most effective proof points and objections
- All grounded in Harmony transcripts, not guesswork

**Step 7: Build Analyst & Press Narrative**
Create a concise brief (500 words) for analysts and reporters:
- Market context and trend
- Why your solution matters now
- Your positioning and vision
- Key differentiators
- Customer validation

**Step 8: Set Measurement Framework**
Define how you'll know if the messaging works:
- Win rate by messaging version or segment
- Sales rep message pull-through (are they using your talking points?)
- Customer quote frequency in RFPs and reviews
- Time to first value conversation
- Competitive win/loss ratio by message theme

## Output Format

- **Positioning Canvas**: One-page summary of segment, category, differentiator, and proof
- **Messaging House**: Headline, value pillars, proof points, and objection handlers
- **Competitive Messaging Matrix**: Us vs. each competitor, messaging angles, key wins
- **Persona Cards**: Title, challenges, success metrics, key proof points (one per persona)
- **Sales Enablement Brief**: One-pagers, battlecards, email templates, talk tracks
- **Analyst/Press Narrative**: 500-word brief for external stakeholders
- **Measurement Framework**: KPIs, win rate tracking, message pull-through metrics

---

## Example: Driftwood (Customer Data Platform for E-Commerce)

### Transcript Excerpts (Harmony)

**Discovery Call with VP of Marketing, Fashion Retailer (Nov 2025)**
> "We're losing customers because we don't know who they are after the first purchase. We email everyone the same thing. Tools like Segment cost us $50K/year and we still have to hire someone to use them. We need to know: who's likely to buy again, who's churning, what discount brings them back?"

**Win/Loss Interview: Lost Deal to Competitor (Oct 2025)**
> "We looked at Driftwood, but [competitor] felt like it was built for us. They showed us how [competitor] did cohort analysis right in the platform. Driftwood felt like we'd need engineering for a month to set up."

**Product Feedback Session: Customer Success, D2C Brand (Dec 2025)**
> "Driftwood saved us 6 hours a week. Before, we manually exported CSV files and merged them in Excel. Now we just... click. The retention cohort view — that's gold. We've actually fired Tableau because of it."

### Positioning Canvas

| Element | Definition |
|---------|-----------|
| **Target Segment** | E-commerce brands ($2M–$100M ARR) who rely on repeat purchases and lack clear visibility into customer behavior without hiring a data analyst |
| **Category** | Customer data platform (CDP) for e-commerce, not a generic analytics tool |
| **Key Differentiator** | Pre-built e-commerce schemas + no-code cohort analysis → insights in minutes, not months |
| **Proof Points** | 47% increase in repeat purchase rate; 6 hours/week saved per analyst; 3-week onboarding vs. 12 weeks with competitors |

### Messaging House

**Headline**
"Know your repeat customers. Act on it in minutes."

**Value Pillar 1: Time to Insight (No Engineering Required)**
- Proof: "Built-in cohort analysis for e-commerce. No SQL, no data scientist."
- Objection Handler: "But we don't have a data team. Will we need to hire one?" → "No. Our templates handle 80% of e-commerce use cases out of the box."

**Value Pillar 2: Higher Repeat Purchase Rates**
- Proof: "Segment customers by behavior in real time. Brands see 40%+ lift in repeat rate."
- Objection Handler: "How do we know this will work for us?" → "See how [customer] went from 12% repeat rate to 18% in 6 weeks."

**Value Pillar 3: Cost Savings**
- Proof: "Replace Segment + manual analysis + Excel with one platform. Median savings: $45K/year."
- Objection Handler: "Isn't this expensive?" → "Driftwood is 40% cheaper than Segment + the analyst time you're already paying for."

### Competitive Messaging Matrix

| Competitor | Their Angle | Our Angle | Our Win |
|---|---|---|---|
| Segment | Generic CDP for any data | Built for e-commerce patterns | No engineering needed, faster insights |
| Shopify Analytics | Free, but limited | Deep cohort + behavior analysis | Own your data, no lock-in |
| Tableau | Powerful but slow to implement | Fast, pre-built for e-commerce | 3-week vs. 12-week setup |

### Persona Card: VP of Marketing (D2C Brand)

**Role**: VP of Marketing, $10M ARR e-commerce brand
**Top Challenges**: Low repeat purchase rate, high CAC, attribution across channels
**Success Metric**: 15%+ increase in repeat rate within 6 months
**Most Effective Proof Point**: "We went from 12% to 18% repeat rate in 6 weeks. Here's how." (Case study)
**Key Objection**: "Isn't this just a fancy analytics tool?" → "No, it's built so you can activate these insights in your email platform immediately."

---

## Edge Cases

1. **Pre-product-market-fit** (limited customer data): Use interviews with early users, founder calls with prospects, and feature requests from sign-ups. Positioning will be narrower; refresh it monthly as you gather more evidence.

2. **Crowded category with no clear differentiator**: Audit competitive positioning carefully. Focus on a specific use case or persona where you have a real advantage, even if narrow. "Best-in-class for e-commerce" beats "general CDP for everyone."

3. **Pivoting to new market**: Don't assume your old positioning works. Run 10 discovery calls in the new market. You may find customers describe their problem (and your solution) completely differently.

4. **Founder-driven messaging that needs professionalization**: Preserve the founder's core insight and voice; reframe it in customer language. Ask: "What does this mean to the customer?" Translate "AI-powered" to "gets insights 10x faster."

5. **International messaging adaptation**: Don't just translate. Run discovery calls in each market. A pain point in the US may not resonate in APAC. Competitive landscape differs. Refresh positioning per region.

items.name: Build a Promotion Case
items.description: Create a data-driven promotion narrative that demonstrates impact at the next level. Use when preparing for promotion conversations, building visibility, or framing career progression to leadership. Trigger on: promotion, growth, next level, career advancement, readiness.
items.author: Harmony

connectors.name: Slack
connectors.reason: Extract accomplishments and feedback from team conversations and wins you celebrated

connectors.name: Google Drive
connectors.reason: Reference quarterly goals, impact reports, and project documentation

connectors.name: Google Calendar
connectors.reason: Timeline major projects and milestones when creating your impact narrative

items.prompt:
## Purpose

A promotion case is not a plea—it's evidence. This skill helps you build a compelling narrative around what you've accomplished, the problems you've solved, and how you've already demonstrated competence at the next level. Rather than asking for promotion, you're simply documenting the work that speaks for itself.

The goal is to reframe the conversation from "I deserve this" to "I'm already doing this—let's just update the title."

## When to Use This Skill

- **Performance review cycles** — Before your manager evaluates you, have your case ready
- **After a major win** — Momentum matters; capture it immediately after shipping something significant
- **When visibility is lacking** — You've done great work but leadership hasn't seen it; this becomes your case
- **Preparing for sponsor conversations** — Internal advocates need ammunition; give it to them
- **Role misalignment** — You've outgrown your title; document what your actual job has become
- **Transition planning** — Moving teams, functions, or seniority levels; make the bridge clear
- **Compensation negotiation** — Impact directly supports equity, bonus, or salary discussions

## Instructions

**1. Inventory your impact across 3 dimensions**
   - Business outcomes (revenue, retention, growth metrics tied to your work)
   - Team leverage (scaled processes, mentoring others, created tools that multiplied team capacity)
   - Technical/strategic excellence (solved hard problems, mastered new domains, shaped direction)

**2. Extract concrete metrics and stories from available data**
   - Pull from transcripts if you have them: decisions you made, problems you solved, trade-offs you navigated
   - Check Slack, email, or documentation for: features shipped, bugs prevented, cycles saved, revenue influenced
   - Identify moments where you acted at higher scope: cross-functional leadership, budget decisions, hiring/mentoring

**3. Build a "next level" evidence map**
   - List 4–6 situations where you already performed the role you're seeking
   - For each: what was the problem → what did you do → what was the outcome
   - Be specific: "Reduced onboarding time" becomes "Designed new IC onboarding doc; improved time-to-first-contribution from 4 weeks to 2 weeks"

**4. Diagnose why you haven't been promoted yet**
   - Lack of advocacy: Do influencers know your work? Do decision-makers see it?
   - Role doesn't exist: Are you doing the job but the level doesn't exist? Advocate for the level itself
   - Impatience: Have you actually been performing at the next level for 6+ months?
   - Development gap: Is there a concrete skill or pattern you need to demonstrate first?

**5. Craft the visibility strategy**
   - Weekly updates: Make progress visible to manager and skip-level
   - Project debuts: Socialize wins beyond your immediate team
   - Speaking/writing: Document what you've learned; signal authority
   - Mentoring visibility: Demonstrate you've scaled impact beyond yourself

**6. Write the narrative (not the ask)**
   - Start with business context: "Here's what the team needed"
   - Middle with your actions: "Here's how I stepped up"
   - End with sustained results: "Here's what we've done since then"
   - Omit: "I think I deserve"; include: "This is what I've actually been doing"

**7. Prepare talking points for conversation**
   - Have 2–3 stories where you demonstrated judgment, scope, and impact
   - Know how your work connects to company strategy
   - Have a candidate response if someone says "I don't see you at that level yet"—ask what blockers exist and how to remove them

## Output Format

```
PROMOTION CASE NARRATIVE

[COMPANY/TEAM CONTEXT]
Department: [Org]
Role Timeline: [Date started] → Present (~[duration])
Manager: [Name]

---

[IMPACT AT CURRENT LEVEL]

Business Outcomes:
• [Quantified metric]: [Brief story of execution]
  - Indicator: [How you measured success]

Team Leverage:
• [What you built/improved]: [Who benefited]
  - Adoption: [Did it scale? Who uses it?]

Technical/Strategic Contribution:
• [Hard problem solved or direction set]: [Why it mattered]
  - Downstream impact: [Who else benefited?]

---

[NEXT LEVEL EVIDENCE]

Performance at Next Level - Situations Where I've Already Done The Work:

1. [Situation/Challenge]
   - Problem: [What needed to happen]
   - My approach: [How I tackled it]
   - Outcome: [Measurable result]
   - Why this was "next level": [Scope, autonomy, judgment]

2. [Situation/Challenge]
   - Problem: [What needed to happen]
   - My approach: [How I tackled it]
   - Outcome: [Measurable result]
   - Why this was "next level": [Scope, autonomy, judgment]

[4–6 total situations]

---

[BLOCKERS & REMOVAL PLAN]

Potential Concern: [What might stop promotion?]
Response: [What you'll do about it]

Potential Concern: [What might stop promotion?]
Response: [What you'll do about it]

---

[VISIBILITY & MOMENTUM]

Over Next [30/60/90 days], I'll:
1. [Specific visible win you'll ship]
2. [Knowledge you'll share publicly (talk, doc, mentoring)]
3. [Scope/autonomy you'll demonstrate (cross-org project, budget decision, etc.)]

---

[TALKING POINTS - Stories]

If asked "How do I know you're ready?":
→ "Here's a situation where I had to [navigate complexity]. Here's how I handled it."

If asked "What's one area to develop?":
→ "I'm working on [specific skill]. Here's what I've done to improve: [concrete action]."

If told "You're not at that level yet":
→ "I'd like to understand what would move me from here to there. What are the [blockers/gaps] I should focus on?"
```

## Example

```
PROMOTION CASE NARRATIVE

[CONTEXT]
Department: Product @ TechVenture (Series B SaaS)
Role Timeline: July 2023 → March 2026 (~2.5 years)
Manager: Sarah Chen
Current Level: Senior Product Manager
Seeking: Principal Product Manager

---

[IMPACT AT CURRENT LEVEL]

Business Outcomes:
• $2.8M ARR: Led the Analytics Suite product line from zero to 18% of company revenue
  - Indicator: Measured through monthly recurring revenue reports and customer cohort analysis
• 42% net revenue retention: Built scalability playbook adopted across product org
  - Indicator: Tracked via retention dashboards; playbook now used for 3 other product lines

Team Leverage:
• Mentored 5 APMs: Two promoted to Senior PM, three are promotion-track with active development
  - Adoption: Weekly 1:1 learning framework, documented decision-making template used across team
• Created "Go-to-Market Ops Playbook": Documented launch sequence now used by 6 teams
  - Adoption: 80+ launches in past 18 months; reduced time-to-market by 23%

Technical/Strategic Contribution:
• Architected product strategy for enterprise motion: Designed land-and-expand model
  - Downstream impact: Enterprise ARR grew 156% YoY; shaped how sales and success teams operate

---

[NEXT LEVEL EVIDENCE]

Performance at Next Level - Situations Where I've Already Done The Work:

1. Navigating the "Pricing Inflection" Dilemma (Q4 2024)
   - Problem: Analytics Suite was underpriced relative to value; raising prices risked churn; sales wanted lower prices to close faster
   - My approach: Built economic model showing price elasticity; led cross-functional alignment (finance, sales, customer success) with data; tested price tiers with 5 strategic accounts first; created customer communication strategy for existing base
   - Outcome: Implemented tiered pricing; only 2% churn (vs. feared 8%); ASP increased 31%; revenue impact $320K additional ARR in 6 months
   - Why this was "next level": Required navigating competing org incentives, quantifying soft variables (customer satisfaction vs. revenue), making the call when data was ambiguous, taking on accountability across functions

2. Building a Cross-Product Roadmap (Q2 2024)
   - Problem: Product org had 6 lines running independently; customer feedback overlapped; engineering was confused about priorities
   - My approach: Convened the PM team (my first time in explicit leadership capacity); designed discovery sessions; synthesized overlaps into shared platform needs; presented integrated roadmap to exec team with both individual line and platform view
   - Outcome: Platform work became visible; 3 engineering teams realigned; Q3 shipped 2 platform features that unlocked features across all lines; engineering velocity improved 18%
   - Why this was "next level": Required systems thinking beyond my own product line, influencing without authority, making others' work better, taking on implicit org design responsibilities

3. Closing the "Gaps in Coverage" Crisis (Q1 2025)
   - Problem: Two major customers (combined $400K ARR) complained they were "falling through the cracks" between sales, CS, and product; competitive risk
   - My approach: Instead of finger-pointing, I designed a "Lifecycle Ownership Model" for each customer segment; mapped accountabilities; created playbook; led training with sales and CS; built feedback loops
   - Outcome: Both customers renewed with expansion (net +$150K ARR); framework adopted for all enterprise accounts; customer satisfaction improved 34 points
   - Why this was "next level": Took ownership of a cross-org systemic problem rather than staying in "my lane"; solved for organizational misalignment, not just product features

4. Advocating for "Unpopular But Right" Decision (Q3 2024)
   - Problem: Sales wanted us to bolt on feature requests that didn't fit product strategy; we had said "no" multiple times; political pressure mounting
   - My approach: Built alignment with CEO on product principles; made the case economically (feature bloat costs more than it earned); offered alternative (domain-specific integration path); communicated reasoning to sales in their language (pipeline impact, customer lifetime value, competitive moat)
   - Outcome: Sales accepted the framework; we built a more scalable solution; customer acquisition cost improved; the alternative path unlocked 3 logos sales couldn't have closed otherwise
   - Why this was "next level": Required strategic conviction, cross-org influence without authority, making the hard call, taking pressure and staying grounded

5. Stepping In as Interim Director (Q4 2024 – 6 weeks)
   - Problem: Product organization director went on leave; three PMs needed coordination; roadmap was mid-flight
   - My approach: Stepped in without title change; held sync meetings, cleared blockers, maintained team morale, shipped on time; took on hiring interviews for two new APM roles
   - Outcome: All committed deliverables shipped; team felt supported (morale survey +12 points); two high-quality APM hires (both still strong performers)
   - Why this was "next level": Demonstrated I can hold a larger org together, make trade-off decisions at higher altitudes, handle ambiguity without title

---

[BLOCKERS & REMOVAL PLAN]

Potential Concern: "Principal PMs often come from outside. Why promote internally?"
Response: I've proven I can operate across silos and influence the whole org. That's actually harder than owning a single domain. The playbooks and PM culture I've built are part of what makes this team function. Institutional knowledge + capability is rare.

Potential Concern: "You haven't led a team formally yet."
Response: Fair. I've acted as informal leader (mentorship, roadmap alignment, process design). I'm actively seeking formal team lead opportunities. But many Principal tracks don't require line management—they reward strategic impact and thought leadership. I'm building that now.

Potential Concern: "You've been here 2.5 years. Usually we wait until year 3."
Response: I've operated at this level for 6+ months across multiple dimensions. Happy to continue proving it, but the work is already happening. Let's discuss what would accelerate this if timing is the blocker.

---

[VISIBILITY & MOMENTUM]

Over Next 60 Days, I'll:
1. Ship "Analytics Integration API": Unlock customer self-service; reduces sales cycles by 2 weeks
2. Publish "How We Built a $2.8M Product Line" internally: Capture playbook in shareable format; position as thought leader
3. Lead cross-product "Pricing & Unit Economics Workshop": Teach framework to all PMs; demonstrate leadership on strategic topics

---

[TALKING POINTS - Stories]

If asked "How do I know you're ready?":
→ "When pricing felt ambiguous, I built the model to remove the ambiguity. When roadmap alignment broke, I fixed the system rather than complaining about it. When customers were slipping through cracks, I took ownership. Each time, the outcome was bigger than the problem I was handed. That's what 'next level' looks like."

If asked "What's one area to develop?":
→ "I want to develop deeper financial acumen. I've done pricing work, but I want to understand unit economics and capital allocation more systematically. I'm reading [book], sitting in on finance meetings with Sarah, and I'd love to own a P&L on a new product line to deepen that muscle."

If told "You're not at that level yet":
→ "I appreciate that. I'd like to understand what 'at that level' looks like from your perspective. Is it scope? Impact? A specific skill? Let's make a plan. I know what I need to prove."
```

## Edge Cases

- **You work in a role without clear progression** — Map to adjacent roles at next level (IC → PM, PM → Director). Build a case for what the role should be, then step into it.
- **Your team/company is contracting** — Expand your scope horizontally. Document impact beyond your literal responsibilities. Show you're doing the work even if the title won't change soon.
- **You're blocked by a manager who doesn't advocate** — Build relationships with skip-level and other influential leaders. Make your work visible through skip-level syncs, cross-org projects, and public wins. Your manager isn't the only voice.
- **You're new to the level and didn't come up through the ranks** — Document everything early. Get explicit feedback on what "success at this level" looks like. Over-communicate impact in first 6 months.
- **You're in a rapidly growing company where titles move slowly** — Focus on impact and compensation instead of title. The title will follow. Advocate for "Senior" equivalent roles or new levels if needed.
- **You keep getting "not yet" but don't understand why** — Ask for a specific rubric. Don't leave promotion conversations without clarity on what moves the needle next.

items.name: Build a Sales Team
items.description: Hire and scale a sales team that owns repeatable revenue targets without losing the founder's domain expertise. Structure hiring in pairs, build enablement before ramp, align compensation to strategic behaviors. When to use it: 15+ customers closed by founder, repeatable sales motion documented, ready to scale to $1M+ ARR. Trigger on: 'hire sales', 'build sales team', 'sales structure', 'SDR AE split', 'sales enablement', 'ramp plan'.
items.author: Harmony

connectors.name: Slack

connectors.name: Google Drive
