   - Commit to the timeline. Tell your mentor. Share it with your manager (if appropriate). Make it real.

## Output Format

```
CAREER TRANSITION FRAMEWORK

[YOUR CURRENT STATE]
Current Role: [Title at Company]
Years in role: [Duration]
Years in function: [Total, e.g., "5 years in Product"]
Feeling lucky or unlucky: [Assessment]

---

[YOUR METRIC OF PROGRESS]

What I'm Actually Optimizing For:
[Not money. What matters to you? Learning? Autonomy? Impact? People? Pace? Mission?]

Short description: [One sentence that captures it]
Example: "I optimize for learning velocity—I want roles where I master new domains quickly."

How I'll Know I'm Making Progress:
[What does success feel like? What milestones matter?]

---

[GENIE FRAMEWORK - What You Really Want]

Remove fear: "If I couldn't fail, what would I do?"
[Your answer (no constraints)]

Work backward 10 years: "What would I be proud of looking back?"
[What legacy do you want? What will you have learned? Who will you have become?]

The gap: "What's between here and there?"
[What do you need to learn? What experience do you need? What bets matter?]

Reframe: "How do I get there from where I am now?"
[Concrete steps, not "if only"]

---

[ROOT CAUSE DIAGNOSIS - Why You Want to Move]

Is it the role?
• Current: [What you do now]
• Mismatch: [What's misaligned?]
• Solution: [Move within company, pivot function, or new company?]
• Signal: [Does this resonate?]

Is it the team?
• Manager quality: [Honest assessment]
• Peer quality: [Are they pushing you?]
• Team culture: [Good, toxic, or mediocre?]
• Signal: [If only the team changed, would you stay?]

Is it the company?
• Stage fit: [Are you too fast for big company or too slow for startup?]
• Mission alignment: [Do you believe in what you're building?]
• Financial health: [Stable or precarious?]
• Pace: [Sustainable or burnout?]
• Signal: [Would you stay if you changed roles but stayed at company?]

Is it you?
• Growth plateau: [Have you mastered this level?]
• Restlessness: [Are you bored or hungry to learn?]
• Life changes: [Has your life shifted (family, location, priorities)?]
• Signal: [Would changing roles/companies fix it, or is it internal?]

Primary diagnosis:
[What's the real thing? Role? Team? Company? You?]

---

[YOUR DECISION CRITERIA - What Makes a Good Next Move]

Factor 1: Learning Potential
• Dealbreaker-bad: [What would kill it for you?]
• Ideal: [What would be great?]
• Acceptable: [What range works?]
• How you'll assess: [What questions to ask?]

Factor 2: Pace & Sustainability
• Dealbreaker-bad: [Too fast? Too slow?]
• Ideal: [What sustainable pace looks like]
• Acceptable: [Range you can handle]
• How you'll assess: [Ask about average hours, number of on-calls, scope of role]

Factor 3: Manager Quality
• Dealbreaker-bad: [What traits would make it unworkable?]
• Ideal: [What would help you grow?]
• Acceptable: [What's the minimum bar?]
• How you'll assess: [References? Talk to their reports? Gut check in interview?]

Factor 4: Autonomy
• Dealbreaker-bad: [Too much oversight? No decision-making?]
• Ideal: [What freedom looks like]
• Acceptable: [What constraints are OK?]
• How you'll assess: [Ask: "Give me an example of a decision I'd make on my own vs. with input"]

Factor 5: Impact Visibility
• Dealbreaker-bad: [Work disappearing into the void]
• Ideal: [Clear metrics, feedback loops, visible results]
• Acceptable: [Indirect impact, longer feedback loops]
• How you'll assess: [Ask: "How would I know if I'm succeeding?"]

Factor 6: Stability
• Dealbreaker-bad: [Constant layoffs, financial instability, revolving door]
• Ideal: [Runway, growth, sustainable growth]
• Acceptable: [Some risk, but with clarity]
• How you'll assess: [Financials, recent news, company history]

Factor 7: Mission Alignment
• Dealbreaker-bad: [Work against your values]
• Ideal: [Mission you'd volunteer for]
• Acceptable: [Neutral mission, strong everything else]
• How you'll assess: [Do you believe in it? Would you invest your own money?]

Factor 8: Work-Life Fit
• Dealbreaker-bad: [Unsustainable pace, location/flexibility misalignment]
• Ideal: [Pace that allows life outside work, flexibility you need]
• Acceptable: [Temporary crunch is OK; permanent isn't]
• How you'll assess: [Talk to current employees about reality vs. promise]

---

[EXPERIMENT PLAN - Testing Before Committing]

Hypothesis: [What you want to test]
"I think I'd be good at management" OR "I want to work at startups" OR "I'm ready to lead a team"

Experiment: [How you'll test it before full commit]
[E.g., Mentor someone and see if you like it; do a startup contract; lead a project]

Timeline: [When you'll run it]

What success looks like: [How you'll know if hypothesis is confirmed]

Learnings from similar moves: [Talk to people who've made this move. What surprised them?]

---

[SERENDIPITY RELATIONSHIPS - Building Optionality]

People doing interesting work that I admire:
1. [Name, what they're doing, why they inspire you]
2. [Name, what they're doing, why they inspire you]
[3–5 total]

Next move: [When will you grab coffee with them? What will you ask about?]

Companies I'm intrigued by:
1. [Name, why it's interesting, who you know there]
2. [Name, why it's interesting, who you know there]
[2–4 total]

---

[OPPORTUNITY EVALUATION - Against Your Criteria]

Opportunity: [Role at Company]

Decision Criteria Assessment:
Learning potential: [1–10 + why]
Pace: [Sustainable or risk?]
Manager/team: [How strong?]
Autonomy: [Decision-making freedom?]
Impact visibility: [Can you see results?]
Stability: [Company/role secure?]
Mission: [Do you believe?]
Work-life fit: [Realistic?]

Strengths of this opportunity: [3–5 things that are really good]

Concerns: [2–4 things that worry you]

Green flag vs. red flag: [Is this a move toward something, or away from something?]

Decision: [Yes / Maybe / No, because...]

---

[INTERNAL MOVE ASSESSMENT - Before Going External]

Possible internal moves:
1. [Role 1 at Company, why it fits, who to talk to]
2. [Role 2 at Company, why it fits, who to talk to]

Why internal is better: [Credibility, network, benefits, easier transition]

Timeline: [When could you explore this?]

If no good internal options: [Then you go external]

---

[TRANSITION TIMELINE & COMMITMENT PLAN]

When: [6 months / 1 year / Now?]

If 6+ months:
• Skills to build: [What do you need to learn?]
• Visibility to build: [Who needs to know you?]
• Relationships to build: [Who do you need to know?]
• Experiments to run: [What do you want to test?]

If now:
• Financial cushion: [6 months of expenses saved?]
• Notice period: [How long until you can start somewhere else?]
• Job search timeline: [How long could this take?]

Sabbatical option: [Is a break valuable? What would you do with it?]

Commitment: [Make it real. Tell your mentor. Share with trusted friend. Put it on calendar.]
```

## Example

```
CAREER TRANSITION FRAMEWORK

[YOUR CURRENT STATE]
Current Role: Senior Product Manager at Velocity (Series B SaaS)
Years in role: 2.5 years
Years in function: 6 years total in Product (3 at early-stage, 2 at Series C, current at Series B)
Feeling lucky or unlucky: Unlucky. Growing slower. Not learning. Restless.

---

[YOUR METRIC OF PROGRESS]

What I'm Actually Optimizing For:
Learning velocity and working with people I admire. I want to be in an environment where I'm slightly behind, learning constantly, and surrounded by people doing better work than me. Title and money are distant thirds.

Short description: "I optimize for rapid skill acquisition in a high-bar environment."

How I'll Know I'm Making Progress:
• Every 6 months, I can point to new domain mastery or skill
• I'm regularly humbled (sign that people around me are sharper)
• I'm iterating on ideas quickly (learning loops are fast)
• I'm saying "I didn't know that" frequently (still learning)

---

[GENIE FRAMEWORK - What You Really Want]

Remove fear: "If I couldn't fail, what would I do?"
Take 18 months off. Spend 3 months in a startup. 3 months building something myself. 3 months traveling and learning. 3 months working with a mentor on [deep expertise]. Then make a big move.

Work backward 10 years: "What would I be proud of looking back?"
I'd be proud if I became known for [specific domain expertise]. If I'd mentored 5 people who went on to do great things. If I'd shipped something that mattered. If I'd built judgment—the ability to make good calls even with incomplete information. If I'd worked with people who pushed me.

The gap: "What's between here and there?"
• Expertise gap: I'm generalist PM. I want deep expertise in [specific domain].
• Mentorship: I've never really had a mentor who invested in me. I want that.
• Velocity: I want to experience a place where things move 5x faster. I want to know what that feels like in my bones.

Reframe: "How do I get there from where I am now?"
• Experiment 1: Spend 3 months at a startup (contract or small role). Feel the pace. Learn what I don't know.
• Experiment 2: Find a mentor outside my company. Get invested in.
• Experiment 3: Study [specific domain] deliberately. Take online courses. Read. Do small projects.
• After 6–9 months of this, I'll know if I want to make a big move or if I need a complete break.

---

[ROOT CAUSE DIAGNOSIS - Why You Want to Move]

Is it the role?
• Current: Senior PM—I'm setting strategy, leading roadmap, shipping features
• Mismatch: I'm not learning new things. The role has become execution, not exploration. I've mastered it and now I'm maintaining.
• Solution: Need role where I'm 10 years behind the experts. Not a new title, a new domain.
• Signal: YES. This resonates. It's not about going to Manager. It's about going to a place where I'm humbled again.

Is it the team?
• Manager quality: Manager is good, but we don't have a learning relationship. She's not teaching me.
• Peer quality: Peers are solid but not pushing me. No one challenging my thinking.
• Team culture: Good culture. Not toxic. But not a place where people are doing the best work of their lives.
• Signal: Partially. Better team would help, but not the real issue.

Is it the company?
• Stage fit: Series B at slower growth (series A was faster). I want velocity again.
• Mission alignment: Mission is fine. Not inspiring, not off-putting.
• Financial health: Stable. Funded well.
• Pace: Too slow. Decisions take 6 weeks. I want things to move in 2 weeks.
• Signal: YES. The pace is killing me. At early-stage, you move faster.

Is it you?
• Growth plateau: Yes. I've mastered Series B product management. I'm coasting.
• Restlessness: Yes. Very. Feeling like I'm not developing.
• Life changes: Family is stable. Not a driver.
• Signal: YES. I'm plateauing and I need to go somewhere that feels harder.

Primary diagnosis:
It's a combination: You've mastered this role (it's you), the company is too slow (company/stage), and you want a place where you're learning (role + people). You need to go to an earlier-stage company or a new domain where you're humbled again.

---

[YOUR DECISION CRITERIA - What Makes a Good Next Move]

Factor 1: Learning Potential
• Dealbreaker-bad: Repeating the same work I already know. Easy mode.
• Ideal: Role where I'm 10 years behind. Constantly learning. New domain + new pace.
• Acceptable: New to me (different company stage, function, or industry) even if not ideal
• How you'll assess: In interviews, ask: "What would I be doing that I've never done before? What would be hard about this role for me specifically?"

Factor 2: Pace & Sustainability
• Dealbreaker-bad: Death march pace (60+ hours, constant urgency, unsustainable for years)
• Ideal: Fast-paced but sustainable (50-55 hours some weeks, but not always, and with mission clarity)
• Acceptable: High pace for 18–24 months while I learn; then stabilizes
• How you'll assess: "What's a typical week like? When was the last time you had vacation? Do people stay here long-term?"

Factor 3: Manager Quality
• Dealbreaker-bad: Manager is insecure, controlling, or non-supportive
• Ideal: Manager is invested in my growth. Gives hard feedback. Advocates for me. Mentors me on domain expertise.
• Acceptable: Manager is competent and fair, even if not deeply investing in my growth
• How you'll assess: Talk to their direct reports. Ask: "Do they invest in your growth? Can you tell me about feedback they've given you?"

Factor 4: Autonomy
• Dealbreaker-bad: Everything requires sign-off from multiple people
• Ideal: I can move fast on decisions. Can run experiments. Can fail small.
• Acceptable: Some oversight, especially early, but I can prove myself into autonomy
• How you'll assess: "Give me an example of a decision I'd make solo vs. with stakeholder input."

Factor 5: Impact Visibility
• Dealbreaker-bad: Work is indirect or delayed. Feedback loops are 6 months.
• Ideal: Clear metrics. Weekly feedback. Can see results.
• Acceptable: Quarterly feedback loops. Can connect work to business outcomes.
• How you'll assess: "How often do you measure and learn? How do I know if I'm succeeding?"

Factor 6: Stability
• Dealbreaker-bad: Company might run out of money in next 12 months
• Ideal: 18+ months of runway. Growth trajectory is positive.
• Acceptable: 12+ months runway if the learning opportunity is exceptional
• How you'll assess: Crunchbase, recent funding news, talk to people there about runway

Factor 7: Mission Alignment
• Dealbreaker-bad: Work against your values
• Ideal: Mission excites you. Would work there even without a paycheck (mostly).
• Acceptable: Neutral mission. Strong everything else. You're there to learn, not to save the world.
• How you'll assess: Do I believe in this? Would I use the product? Do I think it matters?

Factor 8: Work-Life Fit
• Dealbreaker-bad: Expectation of 60+ hours always. No flexibility. Location is bad.
• Ideal: 50 hours on average. Flex work is possible. Location works for me.
• Acceptable: Some crunch periods (launch), but generally sustainable
• How you'll assess: Talk to current employees: "When's your last vacation? Do you have flexibility?"

---

[EXPERIMENT PLAN - Testing Before Committing]

Hypothesis: "I'd grow faster at an earlier-stage company (Series A) and I'd be better suited to a [specific industry] than B2B SaaS"

Experiment: Take a 3-month contract role at a Series A company in [target industry] as fractional/interim PM or advisor role

Timeline: Start in June 2026. Run through August.

What success looks like:
• I'm learning constantly. Every week I'm discovering gaps.
• I love the pace. Decisions move fast and ambiguity is OK.
• I'm energized, not exhausted.
• I can identify 2–3 specific skills I'm developing.

Learnings from similar moves: [You talk to 3 PMs who've made the jump from Series B to Series A]
• "First month is overwhelming. Second month you figure out what matters. Third month you can see your impact."
• "The financial stress is real. Make sure you have a cushion."
• "You'll learn more in 3 months here than 1 year at Series B."

---

[SERENDIPITY RELATIONSHIPS - Building Optionality]

People doing interesting work that I admire:
1. Ali (Series A founder at [Company], building in [domain], pushing the boundaries on [thing])
2. Marcus (worked at 3 startups, now CTO, knows the technical side of early-stage)
3. Sophia (my old mentor, now investor, sees patterns across companies)
4. James (Series A PM at competitor, 2 years ahead of me in career, knows the space)
5. Emma (just moved from Big Tech to startup, similar transition)

Next move: Coffee with Ali next month (March 27) — ask about his hiring, the pace, what PM role would need. Coffee with Emma in April — ask about her transition and whether she'd recommend the company. Coffee with Sophia in May — get her perspective on whether Series A is the right move.

Companies I'm intrigued by:
1. [Company A] — Series A in [domain]. Founder is smart. They're hiring PM. Marcus knows founder.
2. [Company B] — Series A in [domain]. Raised recently. Ali worked there before.

---

[OPPORTUNITY EVALUATION - Against Your Criteria]

Opportunity: Senior PM at [Company A] (Series A in [domain])

Decision Criteria Assessment:
Learning potential: 9/10 (new domain, new pace, everything is uncertain and ambiguous)
Pace: Fast but sustainable (Series A typically 50-55 hours, with mission clarity)
Manager/team: Strong (founder is respected, early team is experienced)
Autonomy: High (small team, lots of ambiguity, you'd own product direction)
Impact visibility: Very high (weekly metrics, fast feedback loops, you'd see results)
Stability: 6-month runway (raised $[X], spending $[Y/month])
Mission: Neutral to positive (not a save-the-world mission, but domain interests you)
Work-life fit: Could be intense for 18 months, but not death march

Strengths of this opportunity:
• You'd learn rapidly—everything is new
• You'd have autonomy to move fast
• You'd build relationships with founders and investors
• You'd own a meaningful product line from the beginning

Concerns:
• Stability is only 6 months (you'd need to raise more or hit milestones to avoid layoffs)
• Compensation would be lower than current ($170K vs. $195K), but equity would matter more
• You're taking a risk on domain expertise (might not work out)

Green flag vs. red flag: This is running TOWARD something (learning, pace, challenge), not away from something (bad manager, boring work). That's a green flag.

Decision: YES, with conditions. If you can get: (1) manager/founder who invests in your growth, (2) clarity on what success looks like, (3) realistic equity package, then this is the move.

---

[INTERNAL MOVE ASSESSMENT - Before Going External]

Possible internal moves:
1. Lead Product for [New Line] — Velocity is launching a new product line. More learning than current. Less stable (new product risk). Same company, slower pace. Better than staying, worse than external opportunity.
2. Move to [Customer Success] side — Different function, but Velocity's product is slow. Not a good learning environment. Pass.
3. Stay in Product but explicit learning agenda — Negotiate with manager: "I want 20% of time on domain expertise in [X]." Stretch assignment on [hard problem]. This is worth trying before leaving.

Why internal is better: Credibility intact, benefits continue, no severance cost, lower risk, easier to go back if external doesn't work out.

Timeline: Try the internal stretch assignment for 3 months (April–June). If not enough growth, then take the Series A opportunity.

If no good internal options: The [Company A] Series A role is the right move.

---

[TRANSITION TIMELINE & COMMITMENT PLAN]

When: 6 months (move in September 2026)

If 6 months:
• Skills to build: Deep knowledge of [domain]. Learn [specific framework]. Do 2–3 small projects in domain.
• Visibility to build: Get to know founders and investors in [domain]. Become visible as someone interested in this space.
• Relationships to build: Coffee meetings with Ali, Emma, Sophia, Marcus. Build credibility in the community.
• Experiments to run: 3-month contract at Series A (test the pace, the domain, whether you like it).

Specifically:
• March: Coffee with Ali, Emma, Sophia. Start [online domain course].
• April–June: 3-month contract PM role at [Company A]. Continue learning. Stay at Velocity part-time if possible.
• July: Sabbatical. Travel. Reflect on experiments. Decide: stay at Series A role (convert to full-time) or go back to Velocity or find something else.
• If Series A works: Negotiate full-time offer for August start.
• If not: Go back to Velocity, learning intact, experiments complete.

Financial cushion: You have 8 months of expenses saved. Series A role would be $170K + equity. Runway: 4.7 months if company implodes. Acceptable risk.

Commitment: Tell Sophia and Ali what you're planning. Book the coffee dates now. Register for the course. Lock in the contract dates. Make it real.
```

## Edge Cases

- **You're afraid of taking a risk** — Reframe: Staying in the wrong role is also a risk (to your growth and your fulfillment). What's the actual cost of not moving?
- **You have golden handcuffs (unvested equity, sign-on bonus clawback)** — Calculate the real cost. Sometimes it's worth paying the penalty. Sometimes it's not. Make the math conscious.
- **You're not sure if you want to move or you're just burned out** — Take a 2-week break first. Burnout and real misalignment feel similar. If you still want to move after rest, it's real.
- **You have a family depending on you** — Risk tolerance is lower. More important to have financial cushion. Consider internal moves or sabbaticals before external moves.
- **You're early-career and worried about "not making the right choice"** — Most moves are reversible. Go where you'll learn most. You can always go back or sideways. Learning compounds; regret for not learning is worse than regret for making a "wrong" move.
- **You're late-career and feeling like time is running out** — This is real. Accelerate your timeline. Don't wait. But don't panic. Better to make intentional moves than reactive ones.
- **The market is bad or there's a recession** — You have lower leverage. Build relationships and options now. When the market recovers, you'll be ready. Or double down on internal growth if external risk is too high.

items.name: Navigate Difficult Conversations
items.description: Deliver hard feedback, address performance issues, and have crucial conversations with care and clarity. What it does: Structures difficult conversations using Radical Candor (care personally, challenge directly) and emotional safety frameworks. When to use it: Before critical feedback, performance conversations, terminations, or conflicts. Trigger on: difficult conversation, hard feedback, performance issue, conflict, firing, layoff, termination, hard choice.
items.author: Harmony

connectors.name: Slack
connectors.reason: Provides context on recent incidents or communication patterns

connectors.name: Google Calendar
connectors.reason: Helps schedule adequate time and private space for sensitive conversations

connectors.name: Notion
connectors.reason: Stores conversation notes, warnings, and documentation for future reference

items.prompt:
## Purpose

Difficult conversations are where leadership is forged or broken. They require equal parts care for the person and clarity about the truth. This skill helps you deliver hard feedback, address performance, navigate conflicts, and even conduct terminations with humanity, directness, and a clear path forward.

## When to Use

- Before giving critical feedback that might land as harsh
- When addressing persistent performance issues or behavior problems
- During conflict between team members or with peers
- Before having conversations about role changes, demotions, or terminations
- When you sense tension building (a "pinch") but haven't addressed it yet
- To prepare for conversations where emotions are likely to surface

## Instructions

1. **Prepare with Care Personally AND Challenge Directly.** This is Radical Candor. Write down: (1) What specifically do you care about—about them personally and about their work? (2) What specific truth do they need to hear? Don't soften the truth; just deliver it with genuine concern for their growth. Your belief in them matters—it shows in your tone. If you come in angry or dismissive, they'll hear judgment, not care.

2. **Use the SBI Framework to Ground Your Feedback.** Situation (what happened, when, context), Behavior (what you observed—specific, objective), Impact (what resulted—for them, the team, the work). This removes attribution and feeling, focusing on facts. Example: "In yesterday's meeting [Situation], when you interrupted Sarah three times [Behavior], she stopped contributing ideas and the team missed perspectives [Impact]." Keep it to 1-2 recent examples, not a historical list.

3. **Distinguish Your Feelings from Their Attributions.** You might feel frustrated; don't say "You're frustrating me" (attribution). Instead: "I feel frustrated when deadlines slip without communication. Here's what I need from you." This keeps the focus on behavior, not character or personality. Name your feeling so they understand your stake in it.

4. **Stay on Your Side of the Net.** You own your perspective, expectations, and what you need from them. They own their experience, context, and response. Don't assume you know why they did something. Ask: "Help me understand what was going on there." Stay curious. Sometimes context explains everything. Sometimes it doesn't change the expectation, but it builds empathy.

5. **Address the Pinch Before It Becomes a Crunch.** Small tensions—a missed deadline, a short response, changing patterns—signal bigger issues. Address them directly and early. "I've noticed you've been quieter in meetings the last week. Is everything okay?" Waiting makes conversations harder, not easier. Early intervention shows care. Ignoring it shows you don't care enough to address it.

6. **For Performance Issues, Give Crystal Clear Warning.** Don't hide your concerns. Be explicit: "This is a performance issue. Here's specifically what needs to change. Here's by when. Here's what success looks like. Do you understand?" Document this conversation. Give them a real path to improve or a clear understanding that role may not be right.

7. **Separate Decision from Implementation.** If you've decided someone needs to be fired, don't debate the decision in the termination conversation. Make the decision with your leadership team first (with full context and fairness). The termination conversation is announcement + logistics: "We've decided to let you go, effective [date]. Here's your severance, transition, and next steps."

8. **Deliver Terminations Personally, with Dignity.** Never via email, Slack, or video call if possible. In person or on video with good connection. Have HR present. Be direct: "I'm here to let you know we've decided to let you go as of today." Then logistics. Keep it brief. Don't over-explain or invite debate on the decision.

9. **Provide Hope with Bad News.** Even in serious conversations (performance issues, demotions, terminations), offer clarity and path forward. "This role isn't the right fit, but here's what I've seen in you, and here's how I can help you land somewhere better."

10. **Close with Verification.** Ask: "What did you hear me say?" Don't assume understanding. Listen to what they reflected back. Clarify gaps. Confirm next steps and timeline. End with clarity, not confusion.

11. **Document and Follow Up in Writing.** Within 24 hours, send a brief recap of performance issues and expectations (or termination logistics). This protects both of you and reduces "he said, she said." Keep notes in Notion for continuity.

## Output Format

The skill produces a structured conversation prep document and post-conversation summary:

**Pre-Conversation Prep:**
- Person's name, role, tenure, relationship to you
- Type of conversation (critical feedback, performance, conflict, termination, role change)
- Specific situation(s) prompting the conversation (what happened, when, impact, timeline)
- Your genuine care for this person (what you see in them, why this matters, their potential)
- The hard truth they need to hear (clear, specific, behavioral—not about character)
- SBI breakdown: Situation, Behavior, Impact (facts only, no attribution or assumptions)
- Questions to ask (to understand their perspective, context, experience—open-ended)
- Your expectations or outcome (what needs to change, by when, what success looks like)
- Logistical details (timing, location, who else is present for privacy/witness, how long you'll need)
- Potential emotional responses and how you'll respond with empathy (pause, listen, validate)
- Your own emotional readiness (are you calm enough to hold this conversation?)

**Post-Conversation Summary:**
- What they heard you say (their understanding, any gaps you need to clarify)
- Their response and context they shared (anything that surprised you or shifted your understanding)
- Agreed next steps and timeline (with specific checkpoints and accountability)
- Your commitments (support, resources, clarity you'll provide, introductions, training)
- What happens if expectations aren't met (consequences for performance, escalation path)
- Documentation of conversation (date, topic, outcomes, any warnings)
- Follow-up needed and when you'll check in (immediate, weekly, ongoing)
- Anything you need to escalate to HR or leadership

## Example

**Pre-Conversation Prep: Addressing Performance Issue with Derek, Engineering Lead at TechFlow**

Type: Performance conversation (missed deadlines, quality degradation)

Situation: Over the last 6 weeks, Derek has missed three consecutive sprint deadlines and code reviews show quality regressions. The team has had to rework features. Derek was previously reliable.

Care personally: Derek is talented, has good judgment, and cares about quality. I want to understand what's shifted and whether he's supported.

Hard truth: His recent performance is below the standard we need, and I need to see change. If this continues, it affects his role and the team's trust.

SBI breakdown:
- Situation: Last two sprints, Derek's commits came in after sprint end dates
- Behavior: He's been pushing code that failed quality review, requiring rework
- Impact: Team sprint slipped, other engineers had to context-switch, quality suffered

Questions I'll ask:
- "What's changed for you over the last month?"
- "What support or resources do you need?"
- "What's getting in your way?"

Expectations: I need to see on-time delivery and quality standards met within two sprints. I'm willing to help—maybe it's workload, unclear requirements, personal stress. But the current pattern can't continue.

---

**Post-Conversation Summary (Derek, TechFlow)**

Derek's response: He shared that his mom has been unwell and he's been managing a lot outside work. He didn't want to bring it up because he thought he should "just handle it." He acknowledged the deadline misses and quality issues and felt bad about the team rework.

What he heard: Performance needs to improve, I care about him and want to support, but this is a serious issue.

His perspective: He wants to improve. Suggested he take a week off to handle family stuff, then reset priorities when he returns.

Agreed next steps:
- Derek: Takes 1 week off (next week) for family matters
- Derek: Upon return, we'll establish clear daily sync on blockers (first 2 weeks)
- Derek: Targets on-time delivery and quality standards starting with sprint after return
- Derek: Commits to flagging capacity/support needs early rather than silently struggling
- You: Check in daily for first two weeks, then weekly for month following

Follow-up: Send email recap today. Check in at end of week (before time off) to confirm plan. First sync meeting upon return.

Documentation note: This is a formal performance conversation. Keep record in case it needs to escalate.

## Edge Cases

**Emotional Reaction in the Conversation.** If they cry, get angry, or shut down, pause. Acknowledge: "This is clearly hitting hard. That makes sense. Take a moment. I'm here." Don't try to logic them out of emotion. Let them feel, then continue when ready.

**They Disagree with Your Assessment.** You don't need agreement, you need understanding. "I hear you see it differently. Here's what I'm observing. I could be missing context. Help me understand your view." You can listen and still maintain your position: "I appreciate your perspective. Here's what I need to see differently."

**Multiple Issues Stacked Together.** Focus on the most critical one. Don't overwhelm with a list. "There are a few things we need to address. Let's start with this one, which is most important." Follow up with others in future conversations.

**Termination Without Prior Warning.** Sometimes it's a shock (sudden role elimination, company decision). Be extra clear on logistics and next steps. Offer specific support: severance, reference check, job search resources. Keep it brief—people are in shock and won't absorb much.

**Conversation with Someone You've Avoided.** You've been hesitating, the tension is high. Lean into it. "I've been avoiding this conversation and that's on me. I'm concerned about X. I want to address it directly." Starting with your own accountability can shift the dynamic.

**They Ask "Why Are You Telling Me This?" After Critical Feedback.** This is healthy. Answer clearly: "Because I see potential in you and this matters to your growth. If I didn't care, I'd just leave it alone. I care too much to stay quiet."

items.name: Negotiate Offers
items.description: Negotiate compensation, role scope, and working conditions with collaborative language and strategic leverage. Use when you have a job offer, raise discussion, or contract negotiation. Trigger on: offer, salary, compensation, negotiation, terms, benefits, equity.
items.author: Harmony

connectors.name: Notion
connectors.reason: Document your offer details, comparable market data, and negotiation strategy in one place

connectors.name: Google Drive
connectors.reason: Store offer letters, comp research, and competitive intelligence

connectors.name: Gmail
connectors.reason: Attach and reference offer documents in email negotiations; maintain negotiation thread

connectors.name: Slack
connectors.reason: Get real-time feedback from advisors during negotiations (should I accept this counteroffer?)

items.prompt:
## Purpose

Most people negotiate salary and forget the conditions that actually determine success. Compensation is often a symptom: "I'm underpaid" usually means "I don't feel respected" or "I'm not learning." This skill flips the order. You negotiate for success factors first (clear role scope, budget, team size, learning opportunities, autonomy), then money becomes the easy part of the conversation.

The goal is to move from adversarial ("I want X") to collaborative ("Here's what I need to succeed; are you open to discussing it?").

## When to Use This Skill

- **You have a job offer** — Before you accept, understand what's negotiable and what isn't
- **You're discussing a raise or promotion** — Come with data, not feelings; frame it as solving their problem
- **You're moving to contract or freelance work** — Terms matter more than you think; flexibility is a negotiation point
- **Joining a startup where equity is offered** — Know what equity is actually worth (likely nothing) and negotiate accordingly
- **Switching companies** — Your leverage is highest before you accept; use it strategically
- **Renegotiating after a year** — Market conditions change; your value has increased; frame the ask
- **Joining in a recession or slow period** — Other levers exist when salary is frozen; flexibility, title, scope, growth opportunity

## Instructions

**1. Before any negotiation, clarify what success actually means to you**
   - Money is rarely the real issue. What are you actually optimizing for?
     - Respect? Autonomy? Learning? Growth velocity? Flexibility? Health? Sanity?
   - Be honest. "I want $200K" might be code for "I don't feel valued." That's a different conversation.
   - The best negotiation handles the real thing, not the symptom.

**2. Map "success factors" before discussing salary**
   - Identify 5–7 non-monetary things that determine whether you'll thrive:
     - Role clarity: Is the job clear or am I figuring it out?
     - Team quality: Who am I working with? Manager competence?
     - Scope and autonomy: Can I make decisions or do I need approval for everything?
     - Learning: What will I master in this role? What access do I have?
     - Growth path: What comes after this role? Is it clear?
     - Resources: Do I have headcount, budget, tools to succeed?
     - Work-life fit: Pace, hours, flexibility, location?
   - For each: What does "good" look like? What would be dealbreaker-level bad?

**3. Research actual market value (not what you wish, what you're worth)**
   - Use: Levels.fyi, Blind, Glassdoor, Radford, Salary.com (multiple sources)
   - Talk to: Recruiters, people in similar roles, mentors who've negotiated recently
   - Know: Industry standard, company-size standard (Series B pays different than Series D), geography, role level
   - Be specific: "Senior PM at Series B enterprise software in SF" is more useful than "PM salary"
   - If you're underpaid by market, that's your leverage. Use it.

**4. Understand what you're actually negotiating**
   - Base salary: Fixed, immediate, no risk
   - Bonus: Often fantasy; assume 50% of stated to be safe
   - Equity: Likely worth $0 (startup), sometimes meaningful (public company, late-stage private)
   - Sign-on bonus: Covers losses from old job; counts as cash
   - Options: Understand vesting, strike price, probability of liquidity
   - Remote/flexibility: Huge factor for quality of life; often traded for salary
   - Title: Doesn't matter for day job; matters for future jobs; negotiate clearly
   - Benefits: Health insurance, 401k match, parental leave, unlimited PTO (check if it's real)
   - Vacation/sabbatical: In writing, not policy

**5. Identify your actual leverage before negotiating**
   - Do you have competing offers? (Gold standard leverage)
   - Is there a tight labor market for your skill? (Market leverage)
   - Are you replacing someone who left? (Timing leverage)
   - Are you solving an urgent problem for them? (Problem leverage)
   - Can they replace you easily or would it take 3 months? (Replacement cost)
   - What's the cost if you say no? (Their calculation)
   - No leverage is OK. You can still negotiate. But be strategic.

**6. Create your negotiation frame (not demand, question)**
   - Frame as collaboration: "Before I accept, I want to make sure we're set up for success."
   - Ask questions instead of making demands:
     - "Are you open to discussing [X]?" (Gives them an out that isn't "no")
     - "What flexibility do we have on [Y]?"
     - "How do we handle [Z]?" (Assumes it's negotiable)
   - Avoid: "I want," "I need," "I demand," "That's not enough"
   - Avoid email: Live conversation is 87% more likely to succeed. Phone, video, or in-person.

**7. Separate the negotiation into rounds (don't ask for everything at once)**
   - Round 1: Success factors (role, scope, team, learning)
   - Round 2: Compensation structure (base, bonus, equity, sign-on)
   - Round 3: Details (title, start date, flexibility, benefits)
   - Don't present as a list. Have a conversation. Listen to their constraints.

**8. Negotiate for things that are hard to change later**
   - Easy to change: Salary (can go up in 6 months if you're crushing it)
   - Hard to change: Team, scope, learning path, manager, location policy
   - Almost impossible to change: Equity (vesting is locked in)
   - Prioritize: Get the right team and scope. Salary will follow good work.

**9. Build an alternative if they say no**
   - "I understand. Here's what would work for me: [lower ask that's still meaningful]"
   - Or: "Let's revisit in 6 months. I'll prove the value. Then we adjust."
   - Or: "What if we did X instead of Y?" (Trade salary for flexibility, for example)
   - Never walk away empty. There's always a deal if both sides want it.

**10. When they counter, don't immediately accept or reject**
   - "Let me think about it and get back to you" (gives you space)
   - Sleep on it. Talk to mentors. Check if it actually solves the problem you identified.
   - Don't negotiate just to negotiate. If the counter addresses your success factors, take the win.
   - If it doesn't, come back with: "I appreciate the offer. I'm still concerned about [original issue]. How do we handle that?"

## Output Format

```
OFFER NEGOTIATION FRAMEWORK

[OPPORTUNITY]
Company: [Name]
Role: [Title]
Level: [IC / Manager / Director]
Offer Date: [Date]
Deadline: [When you need to decide]

---

[YOUR SUCCESS FACTORS - Priority Order]

What Success Means (the real thing):
[Not the money. What are you actually optimizing for? Respect? Learning? Autonomy? Health?]

Success Factor 1: [Role Clarity]
• What "good" looks like: [Description of ideal]
• Current offer status: [How clear is the role? Any red flags?]
• Your ask: [What would make this better?]
• Negotiation approach: [How you'll raise it]

Success Factor 2: [Team Quality / Manager]
• What "good" looks like: [Description of ideal]
• Current offer status: [Who's the manager? Who's on the team? Do you know them?]
• Your ask: [What would make this better?]
• Negotiation approach: [How you'll raise it]

[5–7 factors total]

---

[MARKET DATA - What You're Actually Worth]

Role: [Title at Level at Company Size in Geography]
Industry standard: $[X] – $[Y] (per Levels.fyi, Blind, Glassdoor)
Company-size adjustment: [Series B typically pays $X vs. Series C $Y]
Geography premium: [SF +20%, Austin +0%, etc.]
Your experience modifier: [Are you above/below average for the role?]
Realistic range: $[X] – $[Y]

Current offer: $[Base] + $[Bonus] + $[Equity] = [Total comp]
vs. market: [Above/below/at]

---

[OFFER BREAKDOWN & CURRENT TERMS]

Base Salary: $[Amount]
• Market position: [Above/below market?]
• Negotiation potential: [High/medium/low - based on budget availability]

Bonus: [%] = $[Amount]
• Market standard: [What's typical for role/company?]
• Realistic expectation: [What do people actually get?]
• Negotiation: [Is it guaranteed in year 1? Tied to metrics?]

Equity: [X shares] / [Y vested] / [Z month vesting cliff]
• Estimated value: $[Conservative estimate]
• Worth if: [Company IPO? 10x? Realistic scenario?]
• Current status: [Pre-IPO, Series X, runway]
• Negotiation: [Can you get more? Faster vesting?]

Sign-on Bonus: $[Amount] (if any)
• Use for: [Cover losses from old equity, taxes, moving costs]
• Negotiation: [Can you ask for one if not offered?]

Total Comp in Year 1: $[Base + realistic bonus + sign-on]

---

[YOUR LEVERAGE]

Do you have competing offers?
• Offer A: [Company, Base, Bonus, Equity, Deadline]
• Offer B: [Company, Base, Bonus, Equity, Deadline]
→ Leverage: [How strong is your position? Can you say "I'm comparing offers"?]

Market dynamics:
• Skill scarcity: [Is your role hard to fill? Tight market?]
• Timing: [Are they hiring fast? One opening or many?]
• Your replaceability: [How quickly could they replace you?]
→ Leverage: [Economic pressure on their side?]

Problem you solve:
• They need: [What are they trying to accomplish?]
• Impact: [How valuable is solving it?]
→ Leverage: [Are you mission-critical or one of many candidates?]

Overall leverage assessment: [Strong / Moderate / Weak]

---

[NEGOTIATION STRATEGY - By Round]

**Round 1: Success Factors (Role, Team, Scope)**
Your opening: "Before I accept, I want to make sure we're set up for success. Can we talk through a few things?"

Factor 1 to address: [E.g., "Role clarity"]
Your approach: [How you'll frame it + question to ask]
What success looks like: [What answer would satisfy you?]
Fallback if they push back: [Alternative framing or compromise]

Factor 2 to address: [E.g., "Manager + team"]
Your approach: [How you'll frame it + question to ask]
What success looks like: [What answer would satisfy you?]
Fallback if they push back: [Alternative framing or compromise]

[4–6 factors to address in Round 1]

**Round 2: Compensation (After success factors are addressed)**
Your positioning: "These all make sense. Now let's talk about compensation."

Current offer base: $[X]
Market data: $[Y] – $[Z]
Your ask: $[X'] (typically 10–15% above offer if below market)
Your justification: "Based on market data for [role at company at location] and my [specific value-add], [ask] is more aligned with market."

If they counter with $[C]:
• Is $[C] acceptable? [Yes/Maybe/No, because...]
• If not: "I appreciate that. I'm looking for something closer to [revised ask]. How do we get there?"

Equity negotiation:
• Current: [Shares/refresher approach]
• Your ask: [What would be meaningful? Faster vesting? More shares?]
• Their constraint: [What are they likely limited by?]
• Trade: [What could you trade for better equity? Salary flexibility?]

---

[RED FLAGS & WALK-AWAY CONDITIONS]

Deal-breaker if true: [E.g., "Manager is someone I reported to and didn't trust," "Role is actually different from description"]
→ Action: [Would you walk away or renegotiate?]

Concern about long-term fit: [E.g., "Company culture seems misaligned," "Growth path is unclear"]
→ Mitigation: [Is there a conversation that would resolve this? Or is it a sign?]

What would make you turn it down: [E.g., "If base is below $X and they won't move," "If manager won't commit to X mentorship"]

---

[DECISION FRAMEWORK - How You'll Decide]

If they accept your negotiation: [What success factors have been met?]
• Do you feel respected?
• Is the role clear?
• Is the team good?
• Can you learn and grow?
→ Decision: [Accept / Negotiate further / Walk away?]

If they counter with [realistic counteroffer]: [Is it good enough?]
• Does it address your success factors?
• Is the gap between offer and market now acceptable?
• What's missing?
→ Decision: [Accept / One more round / Walk away?]

If they say no to major asks: [Is it still a good move?]
• What's the cost of walking away vs. joining with gaps?
• Can gaps be fixed after joining (raises, scope expansion, role shift)?
→ Decision: [Accept / Walk away?]

Your decision deadline: [When do you need to decide? Leave buffer for sleeping on it.]
```

## Example

```
OFFER NEGOTIATION FRAMEWORK

[OPPORTUNITY]
Company: CloudScale (Series B, growing fast)
Role: Senior Product Manager
Level: Individual Contributor (report to VP Product)
Offer Date: March 10, 2026
Deadline: March 24, 2026 (2 weeks)

---

[YOUR SUCCESS FACTORS - Priority Order]

What Success Means (the real thing):
You want to prove you can own a major product line at a growth-stage company. You're not optimizing for maximum money (you're comfortable); you're optimizing for learning and track record. You want to work with strong people and have real autonomy. You've been in slow-growth environments before; you want to feel the pace.

Success Factor 1: Role Clarity & Scope
• What "good" looks like: You own a product line end-to-end (strategy, roadmap, launch, metrics). The line is strategic (not peripheral). You have clear OKRs and autonomy to move fast.
• Current offer status: Role description is vague: "Own analytics product," but it's unclear if it's a greenfield rebuild or sustain mode. VP hasn't been specific about budget, team size, or autonomy level.
• Your ask: Get a 30-min conversation with VP Product before accepting to clarify: scope definition, team you'd have, and how fast decisions happen.
• Negotiation approach: "I'm excited. Before I accept, can we spend 30 minutes making sure I understand the scope? I want to confirm [product line name] is at the priority level where I can move fast."

Success Factor 2: Manager Quality & Support
• What "good" looks like: VP Product is thoughtful, gives you space, gives you feedback, and advocates for you internally. They're someone you'd learn from.
• Current offer status: You've met VP once (30 min interview). She seemed smart but you don't have a sense of her management style or what support looks like.
• Your ask: Have a 1:1 conversation with VP to understand how she works, what support she provides new PMs, and what she expects from you.
• Negotiation approach: "I'd love a chance to talk with [VP] about management approach before I start. What does good collaboration look like? What do you expect in first 30 days?"

Success Factor 3: Team Quality
• What "good" looks like: You have 1–2 other PMs you'll collaborate with (not siloed). Engineering counterpart is strong and trusted. You have design and data support. Team isn't burned out.
• Current offer status: You know there's a senior engineer on the team; you don't know the other PMs or if anyone's burned out from rapid growth.
• Your ask: Get to meet the engineer and ideally 1–2 other PMs before accepting. Gauge team morale and whether they're the type you'd want to learn from.
• Negotiation approach: "I'd like to meet [engineer] and grab coffee with [PM from adjacent team] before my first day. Can we set that up during onboarding?"

Success Factor 4: Growth Path & Learning
• What "good" looks like: There's a clear path to either (a) Director of Product in 18–24 months, or (b) Principal PM track. You'll learn go-to-market, org design, and how to work at scale.
• Current offer status: Not discussed. Job posting says "growth opportunity" but that's vague.
• Your ask: "What does success look like in year 1? What would growth to [next level] require?"
• Negotiation approach: Raise in 1:1 with VP: "I'm thinking about [Director] track long-term. What does that look like here? What should I focus on in the first year to set myself up?"

Success Factor 5: Decision-Making Speed & Autonomy
• What "good" looks like: You can make decisions without 5 layers of approval. You have budget to spend on experiments. You move fast.
• Current offer status: Unknown. Series B often has faster decision-making than enterprise, but if the company is growing and adding layers, it could slow down.
• Your ask: "How fast do decisions happen? Can I greenlight experiments without approval, or does it go to exec team?"
• Negotiation approach: Raise in VP conversation: "I'm used to moving fast. How much autonomy do I have to experiment and iterate?"

Success Factor 6: Comp is Last
• What "good" looks like: Competitive with market; equity is real enough to care (company runway, traction); no shocking gap between your value and what they offered.
• Current offer status: See below.

---

[MARKET DATA - What You're Actually Worth]

Role: Senior PM at Series B SaaS in San Francisco (5–50 employees, B2B)
Industry standard: $180K – $220K base (per Levels.fyi)
Series B adjustment: $190K – $210K typical
SF premium: Already baked into above
Your experience: 4 years at Series B, proved you can grow a line. Above-average for role. → Aim for $210K+

Realistic range: $200K – $230K

Current offer: $195K base + 10% bonus ($19.5K) + 0.4% equity (0.4% is vague—need to know vesting)
Total Year 1 comp: $195K + $19.5K = $214.5K (if bonus paid)
Realistic Year 1 comp: $195K (assume bonus at 50%) = $195K

vs. market: At market, but on lower end. You have room to negotiate.

---

[OFFER BREAKDOWN & CURRENT TERMS]

Base Salary: $195,000
• Market position: At market for Series B. Not below (good), not above (room to negotiate).
• Negotiation potential: Medium. They probably have room to go to $210K if you have leverage.

Bonus: 10% = $19,500
• Market standard: 10–20% is typical for Series B PM. 10% is on the low end.
• Realistic expectation: Assume 50% (so $9,750 actual). Series B bonuses are usually soft/aspirational.
• Negotiation: Could push for 15%, which is still conservative.

Equity: 0.4%
• [This is actually concerning. You need to know if it's:
   - Vesting: 4-year with 1-year cliff (standard)
   - Strike price: What is the option price?
   - Exit probability: What's the realistic scenario?]
• Estimated value: $[Unknown—depends on valuation. At $50M valuation with 4-year vest, it's ~$50K/year if company 3x. Could be $0 if it dies.]
• Current status: Series B, ~18 months of runway, strong fundraising story
• Negotiation: 0.4% is low for a Senior PM. Push for 0.6–0.8%. That's fair for strategic hire.

Sign-on Bonus: $0 (not offered)
• Negotiation: Ask for $25K–$30K to cover losses from your old company equity (if you're leaving with unvested stock).

Total Comp in Year 1: $195K (realistic, without assuming bonus pays full)

---

[YOUR LEVERAGE]

Do you have competing offers?
• You have strong interest from 2 other companies (DataFlow Series B, StreamLabs Series C).
• DataFlow offer: $200K + 0.5% + $20K sign-on. Deadline March 18.
• Leverage: Yes. You can say "I'm comparing a few opportunities." Don't name them, but the existence matters.

Market dynamics:
• Talent market: Senior PMs are in demand. CloudScale is probably hiring multiple PMs.
• Skill scarcity: You're experienced, proven. They'd have a harder time recruiting your replacement.
• Timing: They have a deadline (you're deadline-driven hiring signal). That's pressure on them.
→ Leverage: Moderate to strong. They want you to say yes soon, which means they'll move on some asks.

Problem you solve:
• They're shipping the Analytics product but need a true PM to scale it.
• Analytics is strategic (appears to be 20% of their roadmap).
• Impact: Medium-high. It's not CEO-critical, but it's important for next funding round.
→ Leverage: Moderate. You're valuable but replaceable.

Overall leverage assessment: Moderate (competing offers + their timeline + product importance)

---

[NEGOTIATION STRATEGY - By Round]

**Round 1: Success Factors (Role, Team, Scope, Manager) — Do These First**

You schedule a call with VP Product. Goal: Get her comfortable, understand how she works, and clarify scope.

Opening: "I'm really excited about the opportunity. Before I commit, I want to make sure we're set up to succeed together. Can we spend 30 minutes on a few questions?"

Success Factor to address: Role Clarity
Your approach: "I want to understand the Analytics product strategy. What are the 6–month OKRs? How much of the company's growth depends on this line? And what does 'own it end-to-end' mean here—am I making trade-offs, or is it already decided?"
What success looks like: VP gives you specific OKRs. You feel like the role has real scope (not a sustain position). Autonomy is clear.
If they push back ("We haven't finalized yet"): "When will OKRs be finalized? Before I start, or do I help set them?" → If you help set them, that's a good sign.

Success Factor to address: Manager Relationship & Support
Your approach: "What does collaboration look like? How often do we sync? What are your expectations for me in first 30 days?"
What success looks like: VP describes thoughtful, hands-off-but-supportive approach. You feel confident in her.
If they're vague ("We'll figure it out"): Red flag. This is hard to fix. Ask more directly: "How do you mentor your PMs? What's an example of how you've helped someone grow?"

Success Factor to address: Team
Your approach: "I'd love to meet [engineer name], and I'd like to grab coffee with [PM from adjacent line] to understand how the teams work together. Can we set that up?"
What success looks like: You meet them, they seem solid, and you don't hear "everyone's burned out."
If they seem hesitant ("We're busy"): "Happy to be flexible. Maybe first week?" They'll make it happen. If not, that's a signal about pace.

Success Factor to address: Decision-Making Speed
Your approach: "I'm used to moving fast—testing ideas, shipping incrementally, learning from users. How does that work here? Can I green-light experiments, or does everything go to the exec team?"
What success looks like: VP says "You have autonomy to experiment. If it's big [>$50K spend], you loop me in. Otherwise, go."
If they say "Every decision is collaborative/discussed with execs": This company moves slower. Is that OK for you?

Outcome of Round 1: You now understand if the role, manager, and team are good enough to join. If yes, proceed to Round 2. If no major red flags, continue.

**Round 2: Compensation (After Round 1)**

You're in an email or call with recruiter or VP. You have competing offer intel (but don't lead with it).

Your positioning: "These all make sense. Let me think about the comp. I got [your competing offer number] from [don't say the company, be vague: 'another opportunity']. Your base is $195K. I was expecting something closer to $210K given the role scope and my background. Can we discuss?"

Current offer base: $195K
Market data: $200K – $230K for this profile
Your ask: $210K base (10–15% bump)
Your justification: "I've scaled a product line from $0 to $2M at my last company. That's directly applicable here. Market for Senior PM at Series B is $210K+. I'd like to align with that."

Their likely counter: "$200K base, bump the bonus to 15%"
→ That's $200K + $30K = $230K Year 1 (more realistic if bonus hits)
→ This is acceptable. Take it, unless you want to push on equity.

Equity negotiation:
• Current: 0.4%
• Market for senior PM at Series B: 0.6–1.0%
• Your ask: "Equity feels light at 0.4%. I'd like to see 0.75%."
• Their constraint: "Our cap table is tight. We can do 0.6% but not more." [Very typical response]
• Accept: 0.6% is fair. That's a win.

Alternatively, if they won't move on base or equity: "What about a sign-on bonus to cover my unvested equity at [previous company]? $25K would help bridge the transition."

Final comp agreement: $200K base + 15% bonus + 0.6% equity + $25K sign-on = Realistic Year 1 of ~$225K + upside on equity

---

[RED FLAGS & WALK-AWAY CONDITIONS]

Deal-breaker if true: "Manager seems disorganized or unclear about what success looks like"
→ Action: This is actually hard to fix. Would walk away or renegotiate scope to reduce dependence on manager clarity.

Concern about long-term fit: "Company is moving slower than they're signaling (hiring decisions take 3 months, strategy is revisited constantly)"
→ Mitigation: Ask VP directly: "How quickly do decisions usually move? Give me an example of a recent decision and timeline."

Concern about product fit: "Analytics product is actually peripheral, not strategic"
→ Mitigation: Verify in VP call. If true, either get a different product line or walk away.

What would make you turn it down:
• If base doesn't move above $200K AND equity doesn't move above 0.5% AND no sign-on = too low relative to market. Walk.
• If manager seems disorganized or unsupportive = hard to overcome. Walk.
• If product is truly peripheral = scope isn't there. Walk.
• If team is burned out = you'll inherit the burnout. Walk.

---

[DECISION FRAMEWORK - How You'll Decide]

If they accept your negotiation ($200K base + 15% + 0.6% + $25K sign-on):
• Success factors: All met (role scope is clear, manager seems solid, team is good, autonomy is there)
• Comp: At market. Fair.
→ Decision: ACCEPT. This is the right move.

If they counter with $200K base + 12% + 0.5% (no sign-on):
• Success factors: Still met.
• Comp: $200K + ~$24K bonus (realistic $12K) = $212K Year 1. Slightly below market but acceptable given equity.
→ Decision: Could accept or push once more for sign-on: "I understand. Can we add $15K sign-on to bridge the comp gap?" If they say no, you accept.

If they refuse to move above $195K base + 10% + 0.4%:
• Comp: At lower end of market. They're not budging.
• What does this signal? Company is tight on cash, or they're negotiating hard, or you had less leverage than you thought.
→ Decision: Walk away or accept only if (a) success factors are SO strong that you'll learn a ton, and (b) you're betting on equity. If neither, walk.

Your decision deadline: March 24 (2 weeks). You'll have competing offer decision from DataFlow by March 18. Factor that in.

Final recommendation: [After negotiations, you'll know. For now: dataflow offer seems safer (higher base, more sign-on). CloudScale is higher growth potential. Go with CloudScale IF you can get to $210K+ base + 0.6% equity + clear scope/manager support.]
```

## Edge Cases

- **You don't have competing offers** — You still have leverage (market conditions, skill scarcity, their timeline). Use it. Say "I'm comparing this with market data" not "I have offers." Usually true enough.
- **They say "take it or leave it"** — This usually means they're negotiating with multiple people and afraid of losing you if they move. Call their bluff gently: "I want to work here. Let's figure this out." Usually they'll move.
- **You're joining a startup with massive equity** — Equity is worth $0 unless they get funding and exit. Never optimize for "potential" equity. Optimize for fair cash salary now. Equity is bonus.
- **Role sounds vague or not well-defined** — This is your red flag. Don't negotiate comp for a job you don't understand. Negotiate for clarity first. A bad role with great comp is still a bad role.
- **Company is in crisis or fundraising** — Comp might be frozen. Negotiate for other things: title, role, timeline, growth, learning, flexibility. Salary can come after stability returns.
- **You're moving from a much lower-paying job** — Don't anchor on your old salary. Use market data. "I was paid $120K, so $180K is a huge jump for me!" is not leverage. Market pays $200K—that's the anchor.
- **They ask you for your salary history** — In many states, illegal. Avoid it. "I'd rather focus on market value for this role than my history." If they press: "My last comp was X, but I'm basing my ask on market data for this position, which is Y."

items.name: Define North Star Metric
items.description: Define a North Star Metric and 3-5 supporting input metrics that form a metrics constellation. Classifies the business game (Attention, Transaction, Productivity) and validates against 7 criteria. Use when choosing a North Star Metric, setting up a metrics framework, learning about the North Star Framework, or deciding what to measure. Trigger on: North Star metric, north star, key metric, what to measure, metrics framework, OMTM, input metrics.
items.author: Harmony
items.prompt:
## Purpose

Identify a single North Star Metric (NSM) and 3-5 Input Metrics that form a metrics constellation. Classifies the business game being played, validates the NSM against seven criteria, and defines the leading indicators that teams can directly influence. Grounds metric selection in real product usage and customer conversation signals when available.

## When to Use

- Defining a company's key metric framework
- Setting up a metrics tracking system
- Choosing what to measure and optimize for
- Evaluating potential North Star candidates
- After customer interviews or product reviews that reveal what users truly value

## Domain Context

NSM is **NOT**: multiple metrics, a revenue/LTV metric (must be customer-centric), an OKR (that's a goal-setting technique), or a strategy (but choosing the right NSM is a strategic choice).

NSM **IS**: a single, customer-centric KPI that reflects the value customers get from the product and serves as a leading indicator of long-term business success. You can use Key Results (OKRs) to express expected change in NSM.

Free resource: [The North Star Framework 101 (PDF)](https://learn.productcompass.pm/nsm101)

## The Three Business Games

Before identifying a North Star, classify the business:

| Game | Core Question | Examples |
|------|--------------|----------|
| **Attention** | How much time do customers spend using the product? | Facebook, Spotify, YouTube, TikTok |
| **Transaction** | How many transactions occur on the platform? | Amazon, Uber, Airbnb, PayPal |
| **Productivity** | How efficiently can someone complete their work? | Canva, Dropbox, Loom, Notion |

## Instructions

You are a metrics strategist specializing in North Star metrics and growth measurement frameworks.

### Input

Given the following business context: **$ARGUMENTS**

If the user provides product data, usage metrics, or customer feedback, analyze them directly. If Harmony conversation data is available — customer interviews, onboarding calls, product reviews — extract signals about what users value most, their "aha moments," and the outcomes they care about.

### Analysis Steps (Think Step by Step)

**Step 1: Classify the Business Game**
Determine which game this company plays: Attention, Transaction, or Productivity. Explain the reasoning — some businesses straddle two games, so identify the primary one.

**Step 2: Identify the North Star Metric**
Suggest a single metric that meets all seven criteria:

1. **Easy to Understand** — Clear definition that everyone in the organization comprehends
2. **Customer-Centric** — Reflects value delivered to customers, not just revenue or activity
3. **Sustainable Value** — Indicates habits and long-term customer engagement
4. **Vision Alignment** — Represents meaningful progress toward the company's vision and mission
5. **Quantitative** — Measurable with clear, numeric tracking
6. **Actionable** — Teams can directly influence it through product, marketing, and operational changes
7. **Leading Indicator** — Predicts future business success and revenue growth

**Step 3: Identify Input Metrics**
Define 3-5 Input Metrics (leading indicators) that most directly drive the North Star. Each input metric should:
- Be easier to move in the short term
- Directly contribute to the North Star outcome
- Map to a team or function that owns it
- Help identify where optimization efforts should focus

**Step 4: Validate & Stress-Test**
- Check: does the NSM pass all 7 criteria?
- Check: could optimizing the NSM hurt the business? (Goodhart's Law)
- Check: do the Input Metrics collectively explain movement in the NSM?

## Output Format

### Business Game Classification
- Game: [Attention / Transaction / Productivity]
- Reasoning: Why this classification fits
- Secondary game (if applicable): When the business straddles two games

### Conversation Signals (if Harmony data available)
- What customers describe as the core value they get
- "Aha moment" signals from onboarding or product calls
- Outcomes customers mention caring about most

### North Star Metric

**Metric**: [Metric name]
**Definition**: [Precise, measurable definition — no ambiguity]
**Measurement**: [How to calculate it, what data sources, cadence]

**Criteria Validation**:

| Criterion | Pass? | Reasoning |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| Easy to Understand | | |
| Customer-Centric | | |
| Sustainable Value | | |
| Vision Alignment | | |
| Quantitative | | |
| Actionable | | |
| Leading Indicator | | |

### Input Metrics (Metrics Constellation)

For each input metric:

**[Input Metric Name]**
- Definition: What it measures and how
- Why it matters: How it drives the North Star
- Owner: Which team or function owns this metric
- Levers: What actions move this metric

### Metrics Constellation Map

```
         [North Star Metric]
        /    |    |     \
  [Input 1] [Input 2] [Input 3] [Input 4]
```

### Stress Test
- **Goodhart's Law check**: Could teams game this metric in harmful ways? Mitigations?
- **Coverage check**: Do the input metrics collectively explain most NSM movement?
- **Lagging vs. leading**: Is the NSM truly a leading indicator of revenue?
- **Alternative candidates**: 1-2 other NSM candidates considered and why this one was chosen

### Recommended Next Steps
- How to start tracking the NSM and input metrics
- Suggested review cadence (weekly / monthly)
- Key experiments to validate the metric choice

## Example

**Input**: "We're building a B2B SaaS product that helps sales teams record and analyze their calls to improve close rates. We have 200 paying teams."

**Output**:

### Business Game Classification
- **Game**: Productivity
- **Reasoning**: The core value is helping sales reps close more deals, faster. Customers pay for efficiency and outcomes, not time spent in the product.

### North Star Metric

**Metric**: Weekly Calls Analyzed per Active Team
**Definition**: Number of sales calls that are recorded, transcribed, and reviewed (>30 seconds of playback or summary viewed) per paying team per week.
**Measurement**: Count of calls where at least one team member viewed the analysis, aggregated weekly per team.

| Criterion | Pass? | Reasoning |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| Easy to Understand | Yes | "How many calls does each team actually analyze per week?" — clear to everyone |
| Customer-Centric | Yes | Reflects value received — analyzing calls is the core product value |
| Sustainable Value | Yes | Teams that analyze more calls build coaching habits and improve over time |
| Vision Alignment | Yes | Company vision: "every sales conversation makes the next one better" |
| Quantitative | Yes | Discrete count, measurable weekly |
| Actionable | Yes | Product (UX), marketing (activation), CS (onboarding) can all influence it |
| Leading Indicator | Yes | Teams analyzing 10+ calls/week retain at 95% vs. 60% for <3 calls/week |

### Input Metrics

**1. Calls Recorded per Team per Week**
- Definition: Number of calls successfully recorded and transcribed
- Why: Can't analyze what you don't capture — this is the top of the funnel
- Owner: Product (integrations, recording reliability)
- Levers: Integration setup, auto-record settings, meeting bot reliability

**2. Analysis View Rate**
- Definition: % of recorded calls where at least one team member views the summary or playback
- Why: Bridges capture to value — recorded but unviewed calls deliver zero value
- Owner: Product (notifications, UX), Customer Success (adoption)
- Levers: Email digests, Slack notifications, homepage feed design

**3. Team Activation Rate**
- Definition: % of new teams that analyze 5+ calls in their first 14 days
- Why: Early habit formation predicts long-term retention
- Owner: Growth, Customer Success
- Levers: Onboarding flow, setup wizard, first-call experience

**4. Insight Action Rate**
- Definition: % of analyzed calls where a user takes an action (creates task, shares clip, adds to CRM)
- Why: Actions downstream of analysis confirm the insight was valuable
- Owner: Product
- Levers: One-click actions, CRM integrations, sharing UX

### Stress Test
- **Goodhart's Law**: Teams could "analyze" calls by opening and immediately closing — mitigate with a minimum engagement threshold (>30s view time)
- **Coverage**: Input metrics 1-4 map the full funnel: capture → view → activate → act
- **Leading indicator**: Correlation with NRR confirmed — teams above median analysis rate have 2.1x higher expansion revenue

## Edge Cases

- **Pre-launch or pre-revenue product**: Focus on engagement-based NSM candidates; note that the metric may evolve post-PMF
- **Marketplace or two-sided business**: Consider separate NSMs for supply and demand sides, but recommend one primary NSM that captures the transaction between them
- **User provides revenue metrics as candidates**: Explain why revenue is typically a lagging indicator and redirect to customer-value metrics while noting revenue as a validation metric
- **Multiple products or business units**: Recommend a single company-level NSM with product-level input metrics; flag if the products are too different for one NSM

## Tips for Best Results

- Provide details about your business model and revenue model
- Share your company's vision, mission, or long-term goals
- Include current metrics you're tracking
- Mention key customer segments and use cases
- Describe the primary value you deliver to customers
- Point to Harmony conversations (customer interviews, onboarding calls) for grounded signals about what users value

items.name: Onboard New Hires
items.description: Structure first 90 days for belonging, early wins, and clarity on success. Listen first before assigning work, create immediate pairing to prevent isolation, define 90-day and year-long success metrics, and build relationship design into early interactions. Trigger on: hire accepted offer, start date approaching, first day, 30-day check-in, 90-day review.
items.author: Harmony
